topics.title: Motivation and Applications of Statistical and Numerical Methods, Significant Digits, Accuracy and Precision
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT865 - STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Motivation and Applications of Statistical and Numerical Methods, Significant Digits, Accuracy and Precision

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motivation: Why Do Engineers Need Statistical & Numerical Methods?

In real engineering practice, we almost never deal with *perfect* numbers. Every measurement we take, every sensor we read, and every equation we solve on a computer involves some form of **uncertainty, approximation, or error**. Statistical and Numerical Methods provide the mathematical tools to:

- **Model uncertainty** in measured data (e.g., load on a bridge, voltage across a circuit).
- **Approximate solutions** to equations that cannot be solved analytically (e.g., roots of $x^{3} - 4x + 1 = 0$).
- **Fit curves** to experimental data so that patterns can be predicted.
- **Optimize** designs (e.g., minimum weight of a structural member under constraints).
- **Simulate** real-world systems where closed-form solutions do not exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 sets the foundation. Mastery of *error terminology* and *significant digits* is mandatory because every numerical method you study later (root finding, interpolation, integration, ODEs) inherits these definitions directly.

## 1.2 Applications of Statistical and Numerical Methods

| Engineering Domain | Typical Statistical/Numerical Task |
|---|---|
| Civil Engineering | Reliability analysis of structures, curve fitting of load-test data |
| Mechanical Engineering | Finite Element Method (FEM) for stress analysis, thermal simulation |
| Electrical Engineering | Signal noise filtering, FFT-based spectral analysis |
| Computer Science | Machine learning regression, numerical optimization |
| Chemical Engineering | Process control, reaction kinetics modeling |
| Biomedical Engineering | ECG/EEG signal processing, statistical clinical trials |

## 1.3 Significant Digits — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** The **significant digits** (or *significant figures*) of a number are the digits that carry meaningful information about its precision. This includes all **non-zero digits**, all **zeros between non-zero digits**, and **trailing zeros after the decimal point**. Leading zeros are *never* significant.

**Example:** The number **0.0040520** has **5 significant figures** (4, 0, 5, 2, 0). The leading zeros (0.00) are placeholders only.

## 1.4 Accuracy and Precision — Formal Definition

- **Accuracy:** How *close* a measured/computed value is to the **true value** (theoretical or accepted reference).
- **Precision:** How *close* repeated measurements are to **each other**, regardless of whether they hit the true value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Archery / Darts):**
> Imagine four darts thrown at a dartboard:
> - **Accurate & Precise** — All darts land tightly clustered in the bullseye.
> - **Precise but Inaccurate** — All darts land tightly clustered, but far from the bullseye (systematic error present).
> - **Accurate but Imprecise** — Darts scatter around the bullseye on average (random error present).
> - **Neither** — Darts scatter wildly far from the bullseye.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision depends on the instrument's resolution; accuracy depends on its calibration.** A digital scale reading **65.4321 g** is highly precise, but if it is mis-calibrated, it is not accurate.

## 1.5 Types of Numerical Errors (Preview for Module 1)

- **Inherent Errors** — Originate from the physical measurement (instrument, observer, environment).
- **Round-off Errors** — Caused by representing a number with a finite number of digits (e.g., $\pi \approx 3.1416$).
- **Truncation Errors** — Caused by terminating an infinite mathematical process early (e.g., using only 4 terms of a Taylor series).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Standard Rules for Counting Significant Digits

Step-by-step logic applied to *any* decimal number:

1. **All non-zero digits are significant.**
   - *Why:* They unambiguously contribute to the precision of the value.
   - Example: $523.4$ has **4 sig figs**.

2. **Zeros between two non-zero digits are significant.**
   - *Why:* They hold a place between meaningful digits.
   - Example: $5007$ has **4 sig figs**.

3. **Leading zeros are NOT significant.**
   - *Why:* They exist only to fix the decimal position (placeholder).
   - Example: $0.0042$ has **2 sig figs**.

4. **Trailing zeros to the right of a decimal point ARE significant.**
   - *Why:* They express measured precision beyond the decimal.
   - Example: $42.00$ has **4 sig figs**.

5. **Trailing zeros in a whole number (no decimal shown) are ambiguous.**
   - *Why:* $1500$ could have 2, 3, or 4 sig figs. Use scientific notation to clarify:
     $1.50 \times 10^{3}$ → **3 sig figs**.

## 2.2 Rounding Rules (KTU Standard — "Round Half Up" with 5-Rule)

When reducing a number to $n$ significant digits:

- If the digit to be dropped is **less than 5** → keep the previous digit unchanged.
- If the digit to be dropped is **greater than 5** → increase the previous digit by 1.
- If the digit to be dropped is **exactly 5** followed by non-zero digits → round up.
- If the digit to be dropped is **exactly 5** with no following non-zero digits (Banker's rounding tie) → round to the nearest **even** digit. *KTU typically follows the simpler "round half up" rule.*

## 2.3 Numerical Measures of Error

Let $x_T$ = true value and $x_A$ = approximate (computed/measured) value.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Name | Formula | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $E_a$ | Absolute Error | $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$ | Magnitude of error in original units |
| $E_r$ | Relative Error | $E_r = \dfrac{E_a}{\vert x_T \vert}$ | Normalized error (dimensionless) |
| $E_p$ | Percentage Error | $E_p = E_r \times 100\,\%$ | Reporting error as a percentage |
| $\epsilon_t$ | True Percent Relative Error | $\epsilon_t = \dfrac{x_{\text{true}} - x_{\text{approx}}}{x_{\text{true}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Comparing approximations |
| $\epsilon_a$ | Approximate Percent Relative Error | $\epsilon_a = \dfrac{x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}}}{x_{\text{new}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Stopping criterion in iterative methods |
| $x_{\text{fl}}(x)$ | Floating-point representation | $x_{\text{fl}} = \pm 0.d_1 d_2 \dots d_t \times \beta^{e}$ | IEEE-754 normalized form |

## 2.4 Floating-Point Number Representation (Conceptual)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Any non-zero real number in a digital computer is stored as:
> $$\pm \, 0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \ldots d_t \times \beta^{e}$$
> where $d_1 \neq 0$, $\beta$ is the base (typically **2** in IEEE-754), $t$ is the number of mantissa digits (precision), and $e$ is the exponent. This is called the **normalized scientific form**.

**Real-world utility:** This is exactly how MATLAB, Python NumPy, and C `double` store numbers. Understanding $t$ and $\beta$ explains why catastrophic cancellation and round-off errors occur in long calculations.

## 2.5 Relationship Between Accuracy and Precision

- **High accuracy ⇒ low systematic error (bias).**
- **High precision ⇒ low random error (scatter).**
- Improving precision requires better instruments/repeatability.
- Improving accuracy requires calibration and bias correction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example 1 — Counting Significant Figures

**Problem:** Determine the number of significant figures in each:
(a) $0.005240$ (b) $1.30 \times 10^{4}$ (c) $7500.0$

### Solution

**(a)** $0.005240$

Step 1 — Apply Rule 3: Leading zeros are **not** significant.
  - The $0.00$ before $5$ are placeholders only.

Step 2 — Apply Rule 1: $5, 2, 4$ are non-zero ⇒ significant.

Step 3 — Apply Rule 4: Trailing zero after the decimal is significant.

$$\text{Sig figs in } 0.005240 = \mathbf{4} \;\; (5, 2, 4, 0)$$

**(b)** $1.30 \times 10^{4}$

Step 1 — Scientific notation makes zeros unambiguous.
Step 2 — $1, 3$ are non-zero significant. The trailing $0$ is to the right of a decimal point ⇒ significant.

$$\text{Sig figs} = \mathbf{3} \;\; (1, 3, 0)$$

**(c)** $7500.0$

Step 1 — The decimal point is present, so **all** trailing zeros are significant.

$$\text{Sig figs} = \mathbf{5} \;\; (7, 5, 0, 0, 0)$$

## 3.2 Example 2 — Rounding to Specified Significant Digits

**Problem:** Round $x = 3.14159265$ to (i) 4 sig figs, (ii) 6 sig figs.

### Solution

**(i) To 4 significant figures:**

We keep digits $3, 1, 4, 1$. The next digit is **5** followed by non-zero digits ($9265\ldots$), so we round **up**.

$$x \approx \mathbf{3.142}$$

**(ii) To 6 significant figures:**

We keep digits $3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9$. The next digit is $2 < 5$, so we keep the previous digit unchanged.

$$x \approx \mathbf{3.14159}$$

## 3.3 Example 3 — Computing True and Approximate Errors

**Problem:** The true value of $\sqrt{2} = 1.41421356\ldots$ A student approximates it as $x_A = 1.41$. Compute (a) absolute error, (b) relative error, (c) percentage relative error.

### Solution

**(a) Absolute Error:**

$$E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert = \vert 1.41421356 - 1.41 \vert = \mathbf{0.00421356}$$

**(b) Relative Error:**

$$E_r = \dfrac{E_a}{\vert x_T \vert} = \dfrac{0.00421356}{1.41421356} \approx \mathbf{0.00298}$$

**(c) Percentage Relative Error:**

$$E_p = E_r \times 100 = 0.00298 \times 100 \approx \mathbf{0.298\,\%}$$

## 3.4 Example 4 — Stopping Criterion in an Iterative Method

**Problem:** An iteration gives successive approximations: $x_0 = 1.0$, $x_1 = 1.1$, $x_2 = 1.108$. Compute $\epsilon_a$ at step 2.

### Solution

$$\epsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}}}{x_{\text{new}}} \right\vert \times 100\,\%$$

$$\epsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{1.108 - 1.100}{1.108} \right\vert \times 100\,\%$$

$$\epsilon_a = \left\vert \dfrac{0.008}{1.108} \right\vert \times 100\,\% \approx \mathbf{0.722\,\%}$$

If the pre-set tolerance is $\epsilon_s = 0.5\,\%$, the iteration must continue.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Accuracy vs Precision

| Scenario | Cluster Tight? | On Target? | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| S1 — Tight cluster, on target | Yes | Yes | High | High |
| S2 — Tight cluster, off target | Yes | No | Low | High |
| S3 — Scattered, around target | No | Yes (on average) | High | Low |
| S4 — Scattered, off target | No | No | Low | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Concept Map — Module 1 Foundations

The following Mermaid flow chart shows the logical hierarchy and interrelation of the core concepts introduced in Module 1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Real Engineering Problem] --> startB[Need for Mathematical Tools]
    startB --> startC[Statistical and Numerical Methods]

    startC --> node1[Statistical Methods]
    startC --> node2[Numerical Methods]

    node1 --> node1a[Data Analysis]
    node1 --> node1b[Error Modeling]
    node1 --> node1c[Regression and Optimization]

    node2 --> node2a[Root Finding]
    node2 --> node2b[Interpolation]
    node2 --> node2c[Numerical Integration]
    node2 --> node2d[ODE Solvers]

    node2a --> errA[Underlying Error Theory]
    node2b --> errA
    node2c --> errA
    node2d --> errA

    errA --> errA1[Significant Digits]
    errA --> errA2[Accuracy and Precision]
    errA --> errA3[Round off and Truncation Errors]
    errA --> errA4[Floating Point Representation]

    errA1 --> ktu1[KTU Board Exam Focus]
    errA2 --> ktu1
    errA3 --> ktu1
    errA4 --> ktu1
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Error Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Measured or Computed Value xA] --> procA[Compare with True Value xT]
    xTref[Reference True Value xT] --> procA
    procA --> procB[Compute Absolute Error Ea]
    procB --> procC[Compute Relative Error Er]
    procC --> procD[Compute Percentage Error Ep]
    procD --> out1[Report and Decide Stopping Criterion]

    inpA --> procE[Count Significant Digits]
    procE --> procF[Apply Rounding Rule]
    procF --> out2[Final Reported Result with Proper Precision]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note for Students:** Every numerical method you will study (Newton-Raphson, Simpson's Rule, Runge-Kutta, etc.) ultimately feeds into the right-hand block `out1` (stopping criterion) and `out2` (significant-figure reporting). Module 1 concepts are therefore *reused* in every later module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between accuracy and precision. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Accuracy | Precision |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Closeness to the **true value** | Closeness of repeated readings to **each other** |
| Affected by | Calibration, systematic error | Instrument resolution, random error |
| Engineering Example | A thermometer reads **99.8 °C** when water boils at 100 °C | Five trials give **72.4, 72.5, 72.4, 72.5, 72.4** °C — very precise but possibly inaccurate |

*Self-explanatory example:* A clock that loses exactly 5 minutes every day is **inaccurate but precise** (every reading agrees with the last).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the rules for identifying significant digits. How many significant figures are there in $0.01040$?**

**Model Answer:**

Five rules (as stated in Section 2.1):

1. All non-zero digits are significant.
2. Zeros between non-zero digits are significant.
3. Leading zeros are **not** significant.
4. Trailing zeros after a decimal point **are** significant.
5. Trailing zeros in a whole number (no decimal shown) are ambiguous — use scientific notation.

**Counting for $0.01040$:**

- Leading zeros $0.0$ → **not** significant.
- $1, 0, 4, 0$ → all significant ($0$ is between non-zero digits; trailing $0$ after the decimal is significant).

$$\text{Number of significant figures} = \mathbf{4}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *leading zeros* with *significant zeros*. Many students lose 2 marks by counting leading zeros in $0.0042$ as significant.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the absolute value bars in the error formula $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$. Error is **always non-negative**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In percentage error, multiplying by 100 and forgetting the **%** sign in the final answer.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using raw $x_T - x_A$ in $E_r$ without absolute value — *relative error is always positive.*
> - **Allocation Tip:** In KTU valuation, part (a) carries the *concept* (≈7 marks) and part (b) carries the *numerical computation* (≈7 marks). State definitions clearly before plugging in numbers.

---

### Q.A. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the following terms with one example each:
(i) Absolute error, (ii) Relative error, (iii) Percentage error, (iv) Truncation error, (v) Round-off error.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The true value of a physical quantity is $24.762$. Three experimental measurements are $24.5$, $24.9$, and $25.1$. Compute the mean absolute error, relative error, and percentage error of the average measurement.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

(i) **Absolute Error:** $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$
Example: True length $= 10.00$ cm, measured $= 9.98$ cm $\Rightarrow E_a = 0.02$ cm.

(ii) **Relative Error:** $E_r = E_a / \vert x_T \vert$ — dimensionless.
Example: Above $\Rightarrow E_r = 0.02/10.00 = 0.002$.

(iii) **Percentage Error:** $E_p = E_r \times 100\,\%$.
Example: $E_p = 0.2\,\%$.

(iv) **Truncation Error:** Error caused by terminating a mathematical process early, e.g., using only the first 3 terms of an infinite Taylor series for $e^x$.

(v) **Round-off Error:** Error caused by storing a number with finite precision, e.g., $1/3 \approx 0.3333$ instead of $0.333\ldots$

**Part (b):**

Step 1 — Compute the mean of the three measurements:

$$\bar{x}_A = \dfrac{24.5 + 24.9 + 25.1}{3} = \dfrac{74.5}{3} = 24.833\ldots$$

Step 2 — Compute the absolute error using the mean:

$$E_a = \vert x_T - \bar{x}_A \vert = \vert 24.762 - 24.833 \vert = 0.071$$

Step 3 — Compute relative error:

$$E_r = \dfrac{0.071}{24.762} \approx 0.002868$$

Step 4 — Compute percentage error:

$$E_p = 0.002868 \times 100 \approx 0.287\,\%$$

$$\boxed{E_a \approx 0.071, \quad E_r \approx 2.868 \times 10^{-3}, \quad E_p \approx 0.287\,\%}$$

---

### Q.B. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What are significant figures? Explain the five rules for counting significant figures with suitable examples. Discuss the ambiguity in writing the number $1500$ and show how scientific notation resolves it.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Round the number $\pi = 3.1415926535\ldots$ to (i) 3 significant figures, (ii) 5 significant figures. State the rounding rule applied in each case. If a measurement is reported as $4.50 \times 10^{3}$ m, what is the range of values it represents?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Significant figures are the digits in a number that carry meaningful information about its precision (definition as in Section 1.3).

**Five Rules with Examples:** *(Refer to Section 2.1 for the full list)*

- Rule 1: $523.4$ → 4 sig figs.
- Rule 2: $5007$ → 4 sig figs.
- Rule 3: $0.0042$ → 2 sig figs.
- Rule 4: $42.00$ → 4 sig figs.
- Rule 5: $1500$ → ambiguous (2, 3, or 4 sig figs). Scientific notation removes ambiguity:
  - $1.5 \times 10^{3}$ → 2 sig figs.
  - $1.50 \times 10^{3}$ → 3 sig figs.
  - $1.500 \times 10^{3}$ → 4 sig figs.

**Part (b):**

(i) **To 3 sig figs:**

We keep $3, 1, 4$. The next digit is $1 < 5$, so we keep the previous digit unchanged.

$$\pi \approx \mathbf{3.14}$$

(ii) **To 5 sig figs:**

We keep $3, 1, 4, 1, 5$. The next digit is $9 > 5$, so we round **up**.

$$\pi \approx \mathbf{3.1416}$$

**Range represented by $4.50 \times 10^{3}$ m:**

Since the number has **3 significant figures**, the last significant digit (the zero in the hundredths place of the mantissa) is uncertain by $\pm 0.01 \times 10^{3} = \pm 10$ m.

$$\text{Range}: \;\; 4500 - 10 \leq x \leq 4500 + 10$$

$$\boxed{4490 \text{ m} \;\leq\; x \;\leq\; 4510 \text{ m}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1**

- ☐ **Motivation:** Real engineering data always contains error; statistical and numerical methods *quantify* and *propagate* them.
- ☐ **Significant Figures:** Count non-zero digits, internal zeros, and trailing zeros after decimal; ignore leading zeros.
- ☐ **Ambiguity Fix:** Use scientific notation $a \times 10^{n}$ with $1 \leq a < 10$ for whole numbers with trailing zeros.
- ☐ **Accuracy vs Precision:** Accuracy = closeness to **true** value; Precision = closeness among **repeated** values.
- ☐ **Error Formulas (memorize):**
  - $E_a = \vert x_T - x_A \vert$
  - $E_r = E_a / \vert x_T \vert$
  - $E_p = E_r \times 100\,\%$
- ☐ **Approximate Error (iterative stopping):** $\epsilon_a = \vert (x_{\text{new}} - x_{\text{old}}) / x_{\text{new}} \vert \times 100\,\%$
- ☐ **Rounding Rule:** If next digit $< 5$ keep; if $> 5$ increment; if $= 5$ followed by non-zero, round up (KTU default).
- ☐ **Floating-Point Form:** $\pm 0.d_1 d_2 \ldots d_t \times \beta^{e}$, with $\beta = 2$ for IEEE-754.
- ☐ **Two Error Sources:** Round-off (finite precision storage) and Truncation (terminating infinite processes).
- ☐ **High-Yield Tip for KTU:** Always carry absolute-value bars in $E_a$ and $E_r$; explicitly state units; report final answers with the same number of significant figures as the input with the *least* precision.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Truncation and Round-off Errors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT865 - STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Truncation and Round-off Errors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Truncation Error (Mathematical Approximation Error)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Truncation Error** is the error introduced when an infinite mathematical process (such as an infinite series, a continuous integral, or a derivative) is replaced by a finite approximation. It arises because we *cut off* (truncate) the higher-order terms of a mathematical expression to make it computationally tractable.

Mathematically, if the exact value is $E$ and the truncated approximation is $A$, then:

$$E_T = E - A$$

where $E_T$ is the truncation error.

### 1.2 Round-off Error (Computational Representation Error)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Round-off Error** is the error that occurs because computers can only represent real numbers using a **finite number of digits**. A real number must be stored in a fixed-length binary word (e.g., 32-bit or 64-bit), so any infinitely-long or non-terminating expansion is *rounded* to the nearest representable machine number.

If the true value is $x$ and the stored machine approximation is $fl(x)$, then:

$$E_R = fl(x) - x$$

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Ruler and the Map":**
> Imagine you are a civil engineer measuring a straight highway of exactly **1000.000000000... meters** (the true infinite-precision value). You have only two tools:
>
> 1. **A 1-meter ruler marked in millimeters (10⁻³ m precision).** When you measure a length, you must *truncate* the sub-millimeter portion you cannot see. This is **Truncation Error** — you stopped an infinite process at a finite step.
>
> 2. **A digital scale that shows only 4 decimal places** when the true weight is 5.78231... kg. The scale *rounds* the value to 5.7823. This is **Round-off Error** — your measuring instrument cannot store infinite digits.
>
> In a digital computer, **Truncation Error** comes from the math you wrote (e.g., using only the first 5 terms of a Taylor series), while **Round-off Error** comes from the hardware (the finite-length floating-point register).

---

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Numerical Analysis (Highlighted)

The following universal precision metrics must be memorized for KTU evaluations:

- **Machine Epsilon ($\varepsilon_{mach}$):** The smallest positive floating-point number $x$ such that $fl(1 + x) \neq 1$. For IEEE 754 single precision, $\varepsilon_{mach} = 2^{-23} \approx 1.19 \times 10^{-7}$. For double precision, $\varepsilon_{mach} = 2^{-52} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-16}$.

- **IEEE 754 Single Precision Word:** **32 bits** (1 sign + 8 exponent + 23 mantissa).

- **IEEE 754 Double Precision Word:** **64 bits** (1 sign + 11 exponent + 52 mantissa).

- **Floating-Point Base ($\beta$):** Always **2** for binary computers, **10** for decimal calculators.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources and Classification of Errors

Numerical errors are classified into three hierarchical families:

1. **Inherent / Input Errors** — Originate from physical measurement noise or imprecise data (e.g., $\pi$ truncated to 3.14159).
2. **Truncation Errors** — Originate from the mathematical method (e.g., using finite Taylor series, finite-difference approximations of derivatives).
3. **Round-off Errors** — Originate from the finite arithmetic of the computer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Key Insight:** As you refine a numerical method (smaller step size $h$, more series terms), the **truncation error decreases** but the **round-off error accumulates** because more arithmetic operations are performed. This trade-off creates an *optimal step size* in methods like numerical differentiation.

---

### 2.2 Normalized Floating-Point Representation

Any non-zero real number $x$ in a $t$-digit base-$\beta$ system is stored in the form:

$$x = \pm (0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \dots d_t)_{\beta} \times \beta^{e}$$

where:
- $0.d_1 d_2 \dots d_t$ is the **mantissa (significand)** with $t$ digits and $d_1 \neq 0$ (normalization).
- $\beta$ is the **base** (radix).
- $e$ is the **exponent**, bounded by $L \leq e \leq U$.

**Example:** In base-10, $x = 583.27$ becomes $+0.58327 \times 10^{3}$ with $t=5$ and $e=3$.

---

### 2.3 Chopping vs. Rounding (Two Rounding Strategies)

| Strategy | Definition | Maximum Absolute Error per Operation |
|----------|------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Chopping** | Drop all digits beyond the $t$-th place (truncate toward zero) | $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$ |
| **Rounding (to Nearest)** | Round the $(t+1)$-th digit up or down to nearest representable value | $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$ (half that of chopping on average) |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield reference** examiners expect you to reproduce. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Absolute True Error | $E = \vert x_{true} - x_{approx} \vert$ | Dimensionless only if $x$ is dimensionless |
| 2 | Relative True Error | $e_r = \frac{\vert x_{true} - x_{approx} \vert}{\vert x_{true} \vert}$ | Always dimensionless |
| 3 | Absolute Round-off | $E_R = \vert fl(x) - x \vert$ | Bounded by $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$ |
| 4 | Relative Round-off | $\frac{E_R}{\vert x \vert} \leq \frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$ | The famous "machine epsilon / 2" bound |
| 5 | Machine Epsilon | $\varepsilon = \beta^{1-t}$ | Smallest distinguishable unit |
| 6 | Taylor Remainder (Truncation) | $R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x-x_0)^{n+1}$ | $\xi$ lies between $x_0$ and $x$ |
| 7 | Big-O Notation | $R_n(x) = O(h^{n+1})$ | $h$ is the step or perturbation size |
| 8 | Significant Digits Bound | $\vert e_r \vert \leq \frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t} \times 10^{1-s}$ | $s$ = number of correct significant digits |
| 9 | Error Propagation (Sum) | $E_{sum} \leq \sum \vert E_i \vert$ | Linear (worst-case) bound |
| 10 | Error Propagation (Product) | $\frac{E_{prod}}{P} \leq \sum \frac{E_i}{x_i}$ | Relative error is additive |

---

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Practice:**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Tolerances in surveying and concrete strength predictions depend on understanding accumulated round-off.
> - **Aerospace Engineering:** Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) over a $10^6$-cell grid accumulates round-off in every cell — engineers use double precision to keep total error below $10^{-9}$.
> - **Signal Processing:** Finite Impulse Response (FIR) filters must use enough bits to prevent round-off noise from swamping the signal.
> - **Machine Learning:** Training loss curves in deep networks depend critically on numerical precision (FP32 vs FP16 vs BF16).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Round-off Error Bound for Normalized Floating-Point

We want to prove that the **relative round-off error** of a normalized $t$-digit base-$\beta$ floating-point number satisfies:

$$\left| \frac{fl(x) - x}{x} \right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$$

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Write the true number in normalized form.**
The true value $x$ can be written as:
$$x = \pm(0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \dots d_t d_{t+1} d_{t+2} \dots)_\beta \times \beta^e$$

**Step 2 — Apply rounding to $t$ digits.**
The stored value $fl(x)$ keeps the first $t$ digits and adjusts the $t$-th digit based on $d_{t+1}$:
$$fl(x) = \pm(0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \dots \hat{d}_t)_\beta \times \beta^e$$

**Step 3 — Bound the absolute difference.**
The error is at most half a unit in the last place (ulp):
$$\left| fl(x) - x \right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the relative form.**
Divide both sides by the magnitude of $x$, which satisfies $\vert x \vert \geq \beta^{e-1}$ (because the leading digit $d_1 \geq 1$):

$$\left| \frac{fl(x) - x}{x} \right| \leq \frac{\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}}{\beta^{e-1}} = \frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$$

**Conclusion:** The relative round-off error is bounded by $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$, which equals $\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_{mach}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Truncation Error from Taylor's Theorem

**Theorem (Taylor with Lagrange Remainder):**
If $f$ is $(n+1)$-times continuously differentiable on $[a, b]$, then for any $x, x_0 \in [a, b]$:

$$f(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{f^{(k)}(x_0)}{k!}(x - x_0)^k + R_n(x)$$

where the remainder (truncation error) is:
$$R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x - x_0)^{n+1}$$
for some $\xi$ strictly between $x_0$ and $x$.

**Worked Example — Approximating $e^x$ by a Finite Series:**

Suppose we approximate $e^x$ at $x_0 = 0$ using only the first 3 terms of its Maclaurin series:

$$e^x \approx 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2}$$

The truncation error is the Lagrange remainder with $n = 2$:
$$R_2(x) = \frac{f^{(3)}(\xi)}{3!}\,x^3 = \frac{e^\xi}{6}\,x^3$$

where $\xi$ lies between $0$ and $x$. For $x > 0$, the bound becomes:
$$R_2(x) = O(x^3)$$

This shows that the **order of accuracy is cubic** — halving $x$ reduces the truncation error by a factor of $2^3 = 8$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Round-off on a Hypothetical 4-Digit Decimal Machine

**Problem:** Represent $x = \frac{1}{3} = 0.3333\dots$ on a hypothetical 4-digit base-10 floating-point system using (a) chopping and (b) rounding.

**Solution (a) — Chopping:**
The first 4 digits are kept; the rest are discarded.
$$fl_{chop}(x) = 0.3333 \times 10^0$$

Absolute round-off error:
$$E_R = \left| 0.3333 - \frac{1}{3} \right| = \left| 0.3333 - 0.33333\dots \right| = 0.0000333\dots$$

**Solution (b) — Rounding:**
The 5th digit is $3 < 5$, so we round down.
$$fl_{round}(x) = 0.3333 \times 10^0$$

The bound from our derivation:
$$\left| E_R \right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t} = \frac{1}{2} \times 10^{0-4} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-5}$$

The actual error $3.33 \times 10^{-5}$ satisfies the bound. $\checkmark$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Error Type Decision Table

Use this matrix to classify a given error scenario in your KTU exam answers:

| Scenario Source | Error Type | Reason |
|-----------------|------------|--------|
| Using 5 terms of $\sin x$ series instead of 100 | **Truncation** | We stopped the infinite series |
| Storing 0.1 in binary (it is non-terminating) | **Round-off** | Finite register cannot hold infinite 0011 pattern |
| $h = 0.01$ in finite-difference derivative | **Truncation** | The formula is a finite Taylor approximation |
| Subtracting two nearly equal large numbers | **Round-off** | Catastrophic cancellation of significant digits |
| Stopping Newton's method at 3 iterations | **Truncation** | Convergence not yet complete |
| Sum of $10^6$ floating-point additions | **Round-off** | Each addition introduces $\varepsilon/2$ error |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** *Catastrophic Cancellation.* Subtracting nearly-equal numbers (e.g., $a = 1234.567$ and $b = 1234.559$) on a finite-precision machine can wipe out most of the significant digits. The *true* subtraction is $0.008$, but the *machine* result may be $0.0079999$ — losing 3 correct digits. This is **not** a truncation error; it is a **round-off phenomenon**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Taxonomy of Numerical Errors

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["NUMERICAL ERRORS IN COMPUTATION"]:::root --> B["INHERENT ERRORS"]
    A --> C["METHOD ERRORS"]
    A --> D["COMPUTATIONAL ERRORS"]

    B --> B1["Measurement Noise in Input Data"]
    B --> B2["Physical Constants Truncation e.g. Pi as 3.14"]

    C --> C1["TRUNCATION ERRORS"]
    C --> C2["DISCRETIZATION ERRORS"]
    C1 --> C1a["Taylor Series Cutoff"]
    C1 --> C1b["Finite Iterations in Series"]
    C2 --> C2a["Finite Difference Replacement of Derivatives"]
    C2 --> C2b["Numerical Integration Approximations"]

    D --> D1["ROUND-OFF ERRORS"]
    D --> D2["ARITHMETIC OVERFLOW"]
    D1 --> D1a["Chopping or Rounding of Last Digits"]
    D1 --> D1b["Cumulative Drift in Long Sums"]
    D1 --> D1c["Catastrophic Cancellation in Subtraction"]

```

```mermaid
    end

    E --> R
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Error Trade-off in Numerical Methods

```mermaid

    A["SELECT STEP SIZE h OR TERM COUNT n"]:::input --> B["SMALL h OR LARGE n"]
    A --> C["LARGE h OR SMALL n"]

    B --> B2["ROUND-OFF ERROR ACCUMULATES more operations"]:::bad

    C --> C1["TRUNCATION ERROR GROWS O h to n plus 1"]:::bad
    C --> C2["ROUND-OFF ERROR STAYS MINIMAL"]:::good

    B1 --> D["TOTAL ERROR E total equals E trunc plus E round"]:::total
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
```

```mermaid

    C --> C2["CHOP TOWARD ZERO"]:::choice
    C --> C3["ROUND TOWARD PLUS INFINITY"]:::choice
    C --> C4["ROUND TOWARD MINUS INFINITY"]:::choice
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define truncation error and round-off error. State the fundamental difference between them with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Truncation Error:** The error that results from using a finite approximation (such as a finite Taylor series or a finite-step numerical formula) in place of an infinite or exact mathematical expression. It is a *mathematical* error, not a hardware error.
> **Example:** Approximating $e^x \approx 1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2!}$ and discarding higher-order terms.

> **Round-off Error:** The error caused by representing a real number in a computer's finite-precision floating-point format. Numbers with non-terminating binary expansions cannot be stored exactly.
> **Example:** Storing $0.1_{10}$ as $0.10000000149\dots$ in IEEE 754 single precision.

> **Fundamental Difference:** Truncation error arises from *how we write the formula*; round-off error arises from *how the hardware stores intermediate numbers*. Truncation error can be reduced by taking more terms; round-off error can be reduced by using higher precision arithmetic.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is machine epsilon? Write its value for IEEE 754 single precision and double precision systems.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Machine Epsilon ($\varepsilon$):** It is the smallest positive floating-point number $\varepsilon$ such that $fl(1 + \varepsilon) \neq 1$ in the given floating-point system. Equivalently, it is the upper bound on the relative round-off error of any arithmetic operation.
> 
> $$\varepsilon = \beta^{1-t}$$
> 
> where $\beta$ is the base and $t$ is the number of significant digits in the mantissa.
>
> **Single Precision (32-bit, $t=23$, $\beta=2$):**
> $$\varepsilon_{single} = 2^{1-23} = 2^{-22} \approx 2.38 \times 10^{-7}$$
>
> **Double Precision (64-bit, $t=52$, $\beta=2$):**
> $$\varepsilon_{double} = 2^{1-52} = 2^{-51} \approx 4.44 \times 10^{-16}$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Truncation Error Derivation Path**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice A]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] State Taylor's theorem with the Lagrange form of the remainder. Using it, derive the truncation error for the **finite-difference forward-difference approximation** of the first derivative:
> $$f'(x) \approx \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h}$$
> Show that the truncation error is $O(h)$.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Use the result of part (a) to compute the **approximate value of $f'(2.0)$** for $f(x) = x^2 e^x$ using $h = 0.1$ and $h = 0.05$. Compare the two results with the exact derivative and comment on how the truncation error behaves as $h$ decreases.

**Model Solution for (a):**

Taylor's theorem states that for $f$ infinitely differentiable near $x$:

$$f(x+h) = f(x) + h f'(x) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f''(x) + \frac{h^3}{3!} f'''(x) + \dots$$

Rearranging to isolate $f'(x)$:

$$f'(x) = \frac{f(x+h) - f(x)}{h} - \frac{h}{2!} f''(x) - \frac{h^2}{3!} f'''(x) - \dots$$

Truncating after the first term gives the forward-difference formula. The remainder (truncation error) is:

$$E_T = -\frac{h}{2} f''(\xi) \quad \text{for some } \xi \in [x, x+h]$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{E_T = O(h)}$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

The exact derivative is:
$$f'(x) = 2x e^x + x^2 e^x = e^x (x^2 + 2x)$$
At $x = 2.0$:
$$f'(2.0) = e^2 (4 + 4) = 8 e^2 \approx 8 \times 7.389056 = 59.11245$$

**Computation with $h = 0.1$:**

$$f(2.0) = 4 e^2 = 29.55623$$
$$f(2.1) = (2.1)^2 e^{2.1} = 4.41 \times 8.16617 = 36.01281$$

$$f'_{FD}(2.0) = \frac{36.01281 - 29.55623}{0.1} = 64.5658$$

Error: $|64.5658 - 59.1125| = 5.4533$

**Computation with $h = 0.05$:**

$$f(2.05) = (2.05)^2 e^{2.05} = 4.2025 \times 7.7679 = 32.6420$$

$$f'_{FD}(2.0) = \frac{32.6420 - 29.5562}{0.05} = 61.7160$$

Error: $|61.7160 - 59.1125| = 2.6035$

**Comment:** Halving $h$ from $0.1$ to $0.05$ reduced the error from $5.45$ to $2.60$ — a factor of $\approx 2.1$, confirming the **first-order $O(h)$ accuracy**. However, the errors are large in absolute terms because $f''(x)$ for this function is large at $x=2$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Round-off Error Computation Path**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**
> 
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define the IEEE 754 single-precision floating-point format. Show how the decimal number $x = -5.75$ is represented in IEEE 754 single precision. Compute the exact relative round-off error.
> 
> **(b)** [7 Marks] On a 4-digit decimal chopping machine, compute the sum $\sum_{i=1}^{1000} 0.1$. Comment on the deviation of the result from the expected $100.0$ and explain why this happens in terms of round-off accumulation.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**IEEE 754 Single Precision Format:**

A 32-bit word split as: **1 sign bit**, **8 exponent bits (bias = 127)**, **23 mantissa bits (with implicit leading 1)**.

**Step 1 — Convert magnitude to binary:**
$$5.75 = 4 + 1 + 0.5 + 0.25 = 2^2 + 2^0 + 2^{-1} + 2^{-2}$$
$$5.75_{10} = 101.11_2$$

**Step 2 — Normalize:**
$$101.11_2 = 1.0111_2 \times 2^2$$

**Step 3 — Encode the three fields:**
- Sign bit: $1$ (negative number)
- Exponent field: $2 + 127 = 129 = 10000001_2$
- Mantissa: $0111$ followed by 19 zeros $\Rightarrow$ $01110000000000000000000_2$

**Stored 32-bit word:** $1\ 10000001\ 01110000000000000000000$

**Step 4 — Relative round-off error:**
Since $5.75$ has an exact 4-bit binary fraction, the value is *exactly* representable in any IEEE 754 format. The relative round-off error is therefore:

$$E_R = 0$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — First addition:** $0.1 + 0.1$ on a 4-digit chopping machine.

$0.1$ has 4 significant digits: $0.1000$. Adding $0.1$ to itself requires 5 digits ($0.2000$), but the 5th digit is dropped (chopped):
$$0.1 + 0.1 = 0.2000 \text{ (kept)} \to \text{chop} \to 0.2000$$

(For this particular first sum, no error occurs because the result fits in 4 digits.)

**Step 2 — General accumulation after $n$ additions:**
After each addition, the sum grows by $\approx 0.1$. Once the sum exceeds $0.9999$ (which happens at $n = 10$), the chopped 5th digit *would have been* discarded. The 5th digit of $0.9999 + 0.1 = 1.0999$ is $9$, so chopping gives $1.099$ — losing $0.0009$.

**Step 3 — Total accumulated error:**
Each chopping operation after $n = 10$ loses approximately $10^{-4}$. Across roughly 990 such operations, the cumulative error is approximately:

$$E_{total} \approx 990 \times 10^{-4} = 9.9 \times 10^{-2} \approx 0.1$$

**Step 4 — Observed result:**
The computed sum will be approximately $99.9$ instead of the expected $100.0$ — a relative error of $\approx 0.1\%$, arising purely from round-off accumulation. This is the classic *Add Many Small Numbers* problem in numerical analysis.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid These!**
> 
> 1. **Conflating truncation and round-off:** Many students write "truncation error = chopping". This is **wrong**. Chopping is a *rounding strategy* used to bound round-off error. Truncation refers to cutting off mathematical series terms.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the bias in IEEE 754 exponent:** Students often write the exponent field as $E = e$ instead of $E = e + 127$. This single mistake costs **2 marks** in representation questions.
> 
> 3. **Mixing up $O(h^n)$ vs $O(h^{n+1})$:** If you use $n$ terms of a Taylor series, the leading error term is $O(h^{n+1})$, not $O(h^n)$. Board examiners mark this strictly.
> 
> 4. **Skipping the $\xi$ in Lagrange remainder:** Always write "for some $\xi$ between $x_0$ and $x$". Omitting the existence of $\xi$ loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Confusing absolute and relative error:** The bound $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$ is for *absolute* error; the bound $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{1-t}$ is for *relative* error. Writing the wrong one is a frequent error.
> 
> 6. **In $e^x$ series, dropping the factorial:** $1 + x + \frac{x^2}{2} + \frac{x^3}{6}$ — the $3! = 6$ must appear. Examiners deduct for missing factorials.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Truncation & Round-off Errors**

- **Two error families:** Truncation (mathematical cut-off) vs Round-off (finite-storage approximation).
- **Truncation error is deterministic** and reducible by including more terms or smaller step size $h$.
- **Round-off error is stochastic-like** in accumulation and reducible only by higher precision (more bits).
- **Floating-point format:** $x = \pm(0.d_1d_2\dots d_t)_\beta \times \beta^e$ with $d_1 \neq 0$.
- **Machine epsilon:** $\varepsilon = \beta^{1-t}$; the smallest unit distinguishable from 1.
- **IEEE 754 single precision:** 32 bits = 1 (sign) + 8 (exponent) + 23 (mantissa), bias = **127**.
- **IEEE 754 double precision:** 64 bits = 1 + 11 + 52, bias = **1023**.
- **Relative round-off bound:** $\left|\frac{fl(x) - x}{x}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon$.
- **Taylor remainder formula:** $R_n(x) = \frac{f^{(n+1)}(\xi)}{(n+1)!}(x-x_0)^{n+1}$.
- **Order notation:** Truncation error of an $n$-term Taylor expansion is $O(h^{n+1})$ where $h = x - x_0$.
- **Chopping error bound:** $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$; **Rounding error bound:** $\frac{1}{2}\beta^{e-t}$ (same magnitude, lower average).
- **Catastrophic cancellation:** Subtraction of nearly-equal numbers amplifies relative round-off error dramatically.
- **Total numerical error:** $E_{total} = E_{truncation} + E_{round-off}$; both are present simultaneously in every algorithm.
- **Optimal step size:** Exists where the decreasing truncation curve intersects the increasing round-off curve on a $\log E$ vs $\log h$ plot.
- **Significant digits relation:** $s \geq t - \log_{10}(\text{relative error})^{-1}$ for the number of correct significant digits.
- **Floating-point arithmetic is not associative:** $(a+b)+c \neq a+(b+c)$ in general due to intermediate round-offs.
- **Source of "small errors matter":** A relative error of $\varepsilon \approx 10^{-16}$ over $10^9$ operations can produce a final result with only 7 correct digits.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Roots of Nonlinear Equations - Bisection Method, Newton-Raphson Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT865 - STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Roots of Nonlinear Equations - Bisection Method, Newton-Raphson Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Root of a Nonlinear Equation?

A **root** (or **zero**) of a nonlinear equation $f(x) = 0$ is a real number $\alpha$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$. Geometrically, the root is the point where the curve $y = f(x)$ crosses the $x$-axis. Unlike linear equations, nonlinear equations (polynomials of degree $\geq 2$, trigonometric, exponential, logarithmic, or transcendental equations) generally cannot be solved analytically in closed form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 1 demands mastery of **bracketing methods** (Bisection) and **open methods** (Newton-Raphson) for finding real roots. The Board Examiner expects students to *derive*, *execute*, and *compare* both methods.

The general form of a nonlinear equation is:

$$f(x) = 0$$

where $f(x)$ is a real-valued continuous function that is **non-linear** in $x$. Examples include:

- $x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 0$
- $x - e^{-x} = 0$
- $\cos(x) - x = 0$
- $x^2 - \sin(x) = 0$

### 1.2 Bisection Method — Definition

The **Bisection Method** (also called the **Binary Search Method** or **Interval Halving Method**) is a numerical, iterative bracketing technique used to find a real root of a continuous function $f(x)$ within a closed interval $[a, b]$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then by the **Intermediate Value Theorem (IVT)**, there exists at least one root $\alpha \in (a, b)$. The Bisection Method repeatedly halves the interval, retaining the sub-interval that contains the root.

### 1.3 Newton-Raphson Method — Definition

The **Newton-Raphson Method** (also called the **Method of Tangents**) is an *open* iterative method that uses the derivative of the function to rapidly converge to a root. It requires only a single initial guess $x_0$ close to the actual root.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Starting from an initial approximation $x_0$, the Newton-Raphson method generates successively better approximations using the recurrence $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$, provided $f'(x_n) \neq 0$.

### 1.4 Real-World Analogy — The Treasure Hunt

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Check — Plain English Explanation:**

Imagine you are a pirate who buried a treasure chest on a long straight beach somewhere between two lighthouses (markers $A$ and $B$). The Bisection Method works like a *cautious* partner who always splits the search zone in half: "If the treasure is to the left, ignore the right half; if to the right, ignore the left half." This is **guaranteed** to find the chest but slowly.

The Newton-Raphson Method, in contrast, works like a *clever* navigator with a compass and a local map. He stands at his current guess, looks at the slope of the terrain (the derivative), and walks along the *tangent line* to its intersection with the beach (the $x$-axis). He repeats this from the new point, converging very **fast** — but if he starts at the wrong place, he may walk off the beach entirely (diverge).

### 1.5 Why Do We Need Numerical Methods?

- Most nonlinear equations of degree $\geq 5$ have **no closed-form solution** (Abel-Ruffini Theorem).
- Real engineering problems (beam deflection, fluid flow, circuit analysis, thermodynamics) produce transcendental equations.
- Numerical methods yield **approximate roots** to any desired accuracy $\varepsilon$.

### 1.6 Classification of Root-Finding Methods

| **Category** | **Property** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| Bracketing Methods | Always converge; bracket the root | **Bisection**, False Position |
| Open Methods | Faster convergence but may diverge | **Newton-Raphson**, Secant, Fixed-Point |
| Direct Methods | Single computation (rare for nonlinear) | Quadratic formula |
| Iterative Methods | Repeated refinement | All the above |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Module 1 specifically tests **Bisection** and **Newton-Raphson** in Part A and Part B with internal choice. Master the trade-off: Bisection = *slow but safe*; Newton-Raphson = *fast but risky*.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Intermediate Value Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (IVT):** If $f(x)$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$, then there exists at least one number $\alpha \in (a, b)$ such that $f(\alpha) = 0$.

This theorem is the *mathematical backbone* of the Bisection method. It guarantees that between any two points where the function changes sign, **at least one root exists**.

### 2.2 The Bisection Method — Logic Steps

The operational logic of the Bisection method unfolds in the following structured steps:

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose initial bracketing interval $[a, b]$ such that $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$. The function $f$ must be **continuous** on $[a, b]$.
- **Step 2 — Midpoint Computation:** Calculate the midpoint $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$.
- **Step 3 — Function Evaluation:** Compute $f(c)$ at the midpoint.
- **Step 4 — Bracket Selection:**
  - If $f(c) = 0$, then $c$ is the exact root — **stop**.
  - If $f(a) \cdot f(c) < 0$, then the root lies in $[a, c]$. Set $b = c$.
  - Otherwise, the root lies in $[c, b]$. Set $a = c$.
- **Step 5 — Convergence Check:** If $\vert b - a \vert < \varepsilon$ (predefined tolerance), stop. Else, return to Step 2.
- **Step 6 — Output:** The root is approximated as $c_n$ after $n$ iterations.

> **Why it works:** Each iteration reduces the interval length by half. After $n$ iterations, the interval length becomes $\dfrac{b-a}{2^n}$. So the error is bounded.

### 2.3 Convergence of the Bisection Method

The error after $n$ iterations is bounded by:

$$E_n = \vert \alpha - c_n \vert \leq \frac{b - a}{2^{n+1}}$$

The **number of iterations** required to achieve a specified tolerance $\varepsilon$ is:

$$n \geq \frac{\log\left(\dfrac{b - a}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log(2)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Bisection has **linear convergence** (error reduces by a factor of 2 per iteration). For a tolerance of $10^{-5}$ and an initial interval of length 1, we need about 17 iterations. Slow, but **mathematically guaranteed**.

### 2.4 The Newton-Raphson Method — Logic Steps

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Choose initial guess $x_0$ close to the root.
- **Step 2 — Derivative Evaluation:** Compute $f'(x_n)$.
- **Step 3 — Update Rule:** Compute the next approximation:
$$x_{n+1} = x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$$
- **Step 4 — Convergence Check:** If $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ or $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$, stop. Else, set $x_n = x_{n+1}$ and return to Step 2.

### 2.5 Geometric Interpretation of Newton-Raphson

At $x_n$, draw the **tangent line** to the curve $y = f(x)$. The tangent line has slope $f'(x_n)$ and equation:

$$y - f(x_n) = f'(x_n)(x - x_n)$$

Setting $y = 0$ (to find the $x$-intercept) gives the next approximation $x_{n+1}$.

### 2.6 Convergence of Newton-Raphson Method

Newton-Raphson has **quadratic convergence** when:
- $f'(x) \neq 0$ near the root,
- $f''(x)$ is continuous, and
- $x_0$ is sufficiently close to the root.

The error relationship is:

$$\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx \frac{\vert f''(\alpha) \vert}{2 \vert f'(\alpha) \vert} \cdot \vert e_n \vert^2$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Quadratic convergence means the *number of correct digits approximately doubles* with every iteration. This is why Newton-Raphson is the **workhorse** of nonlinear solvers in real-time engineering software (MATLAB `fsolve`, Python `scipy.optimize.newton`, etc.).

### 2.7 Failure Modes of Newton-Raphson

The method **diverges** or **fails** when:
- $f'(x_n) = 0$ at some iteration (division by zero).
- $x_0$ is far from the root (tangent intersects far from root).
- The function has **inflection points** or **oscillations** near the root.
- The function is **non-differentiable** at the root.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bisection Midpoint | $c = \dfrac{a+b}{2}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Bracket Condition | $f(a) \cdot f(b) < 0$ | sign change required |
| 3 | Bisection Error Bound | $E_n \leq \dfrac{b-a}{2^{n+1}}$ | linear convergence |
| 4 | Iterations Required | $n \geq \dfrac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{b-a}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log 2}$ | ceiling function |
| 5 | Newton-Raphson Update | $x_{n+1} = x_n - \dfrac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}$ | requires $f'(x) \neq 0$ |
| 6 | Convergence Criterion | $\vert x_{n+1} - x_n \vert < \varepsilon$ | absolute error |
| 7 | Quadratic Convergence | $\vert e_{n+1} \vert \approx C \cdot \vert e_n \vert^2$ | constant $C$ |
| 8 | Divergence Trigger | $f'(x_n) = 0$ | tangent parallel to $x$-axis |
| 9 | Tangent Slope | $m = f'(x_n)$ | derivative at $x_n$ |
| 10 | Stopping (Function) | $\vert f(x_{n+1}) \vert < \varepsilon$ | residual check |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Structural Engineering:** Solving the transcendental equation $x \tan(x) = c$ that arises in column buckling analysis (Euler's critical load).
- **Chemical Engineering:** Solving the van der Waals equation of state for volume using Newton-Raphson.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Solving diode I-V characteristics $I = I_s(e^{V/V_T} - 1)$ for voltage.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Finding the natural frequencies of vibration: $\tan(x) = \dfrac{x}{1 - x^2}$.
- **Civil Engineering:** Manning's equation for open channel flow: $\dfrac{A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}}{n} = Q$.
- **Aerospace:** Trajectory optimization and orbit determination require high-precision Newton-Raphson solvers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Newton-Raphson Formula (From First Principles)

Let $x_n$ be an approximation to the root $\alpha$ of $f(x) = 0$. Let $e_n = \alpha - x_n$ be the error. We seek $x_{n+1}$ such that the error is corrected.

**Step 1 — Taylor's Series Expansion:** Expand $f(x)$ about the point $x_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= f(x_n) + (x - x_n) f'(x_n) + \frac{(x - x_n)^2}{2!} f''(x_n) + \frac{(x - x_n)^3}{3!} f'''(x_n) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the True Root:** At $x = \alpha$, $f(\alpha) = 0$ (since $\alpha$ is a root). Also, $\alpha - x_n = -e_n$, so $x_n - \alpha = e_n$. We obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &= f(x_n) + (\alpha - x_n) f'(x_n) + \frac{(\alpha - x_n)^2}{2} f''(x_n) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Truncate (Newton's Assumption):** Newton assumed $x_n$ is close to $\alpha$, so higher-order terms are negligible. Ignoring terms of order $\geq 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 &\approx f(x_n) + (\alpha - x_n) f'(x_n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\alpha$:** Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(\alpha - x_n) f'(x_n) &\approx -f(x_n) \\
\alpha - x_n &\approx -\frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)} \\
x_{n+1} &= x_n - \frac{f(x_n)}{f'(x_n)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **Newton-Raphson iteration formula**. The term $e_{n+1} = \alpha - x_{n+1}$ has order $O(e_n^2)$, confirming **quadratic convergence**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Bisection Method

**Problem:** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1$ in the interval $[1, 2]$ using the Bisection method, accurate to $\varepsilon = 0.01$. Perform at least **4 iterations**.

**Preliminary Check (Bracket Validity):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1) &= (1)^3 - (1) - 1 = 1 - 1 - 1 = -1 \\
f(2) &= (2)^3 - (2) - 1 = 8 - 2 - 1 = 5 \\
f(1) \cdot f(2) &= (-1)(5) = -5 < 0 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> The Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a root in $[1, 2]$.

---

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= \frac{1 + 2}{2} = 1.5 \\
f(1.5) &= (1.5)^3 - (1.5) - 1 = 3.375 - 1.5 - 1 = 0.875 \\
f(1) \cdot f(1.5) &= (-1)(0.875) = -0.875 < 0 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1, 1.5] \\
\Rightarrow a &= 1,\ b = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_2 &= \frac{1 + 1.5}{2} = 1.25 \\
f(1.25) &= (1.25)^3 - (1.25) - 1 = 1.953125 - 1.25 - 1 = -0.296875 \\
f(1.25) \cdot f(1.5) &= (-0.296875)(0.875) < 0 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1.25, 1.5] \\
\Rightarrow a &= 1.25,\ b = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_3 &= \frac{1.25 + 1.5}{2} = 1.375 \\
f(1.375) &= (1.375)^3 - (1.375) - 1 = 2.599609 - 1.375 - 1 = 0.224609 \\
f(1.25) \cdot f(1.375) &= (-0.296875)(0.224609) < 0 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1.25, 1.375] \\
\Rightarrow a &= 1.25,\ b = 1.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_4 &= \frac{1.25 + 1.375}{2} = 1.3125 \\
f(1.3125) &= (1.3125)^3 - (1.3125) - 1 = 2.260986 - 1.3125 - 1 = -0.051514 \\
\vert b - a \vert &= \vert 1.375 - 1.25 \vert = 0.125 > 0.01 \quad (\text{continue}) \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1.3125, 1.375] \\
\Rightarrow a &= 1.3125,\ b = 1.375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 5 (for completeness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_5 &= \frac{1.3125 + 1.375}{2} = 1.34375 \\
f(1.34375) &= (1.34375)^3 - (1.34375) - 1 \approx 2.4265 - 1.34375 - 1 = 0.08277 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1.3125, 1.34375] \\
\Rightarrow a &= 1.3125,\ b = 1.34375
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 6 (for completeness):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_6 &= \frac{1.3125 + 1.34375}{2} = 1.328125 \\
f(1.328125) &\approx 0.01514 \\
\vert b - a \vert &= 0.03125 > 0.01 \\
\Rightarrow \text{Root lies in } [1.3125, 1.328125]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 7:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_7 &= \frac{1.3125 + 1.328125}{2} = 1.3203125 \\
\vert b - a \vert &= \vert 1.328125 - 1.3125 \vert = 0.015625
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** After 4 iterations, $c_4 = 1.3125$. Continuing further, the true root is approximately $\alpha \approx 1.3247$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Newton-Raphson Method

**Problem:** Find a root of $f(x) = x^3 - x - 1$ using Newton-Raphson with $x_0 = 1.5$. Perform **3 iterations**.

**Setup — Compute Derivative:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x) &= x^3 - x - 1 \\
f'(x) &= 3x^2 - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.5) &= 3.375 - 1.5 - 1 = 0.875 \\
f'(1.5) &= 3(2.25) - 1 = 6.75 - 1 = 5.75 \\
x_1 &= 1.5 - \frac{0.875}{5.75} = 1.5 - 0.152174 = 1.347826
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.347826) &= (1.347826)^3 - 1.347826 - 1 \\
&= 2.44849 - 1.347826 - 1 = 0.100664 \\
f'(1.347826) &= 3(1.816756) - 1 = 5.450269 - 1 = 4.450269 \\
x_2 &= 1.347826 - \frac{0.100664}{4.450269} \\
&= 1.347826 - 0.022620 = 1.325206
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.325206) &= (1.325206)^3 - 1.325206 - 1 \\
&= 2.327024 - 1.325206 - 1 = 0.001818 \\
f'(1.325206) &= 3(1.756171) - 1 = 5.268512 - 1 = 4.268512 \\
x_3 &= 1.325206 - \frac{0.001818}{4.268512} \\
&= 1.325206 - 0.000426 = 1.324780
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4 (for verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(1.324780) &\approx -0.000003 \\
x_4 &\approx 1.324718
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $x_3 = 1.32478$ (correct to 5 decimal places). Compare to Bisection: we needed 7 iterations for similar accuracy. **Newton-Raphson converges much faster.**

---

### 3.4 Error Analysis — Number of Iterations Required

For Bisection to achieve tolerance $\varepsilon$ from initial interval $[a, b]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &\geq \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{b - a}{\varepsilon}\right)}{\log(2)} \\
n &\geq \frac{\log\!\left(\dfrac{2 - 1}{0.0001}\right)}{\log 2} = \frac{\log(10000)}{\log 2} = \frac{4}{0.30103} \approx 13.29
\end{aligned}
$$

So $n = 14$ iterations of Bisection are needed for 4-decimal accuracy.

For Newton-Raphson starting near the root, **4–5 iterations** typically suffice for 6-decimal accuracy — illustrating the dramatic speed advantage.

---

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| **Aspect** | **Bisection Method** | **Newton-Raphson Method** |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Bracketing | Open |
| Initial Requirement | Interval $[a, b]$ with sign change | Single point $x_0$ near root |
| Convergence Rate | **Linear** (ratio = $1/2$) | **Quadratic** (ratio $\to 0$) |
| Derivative Needed | No | Yes, $f'(x)$ |
| Guaranteed Convergence | **Yes** (within bracket) | Not guaranteed |
| Iterations for $10^{-5}$ | $\sim 17$ | $\sim 4$ to $5$ |
| Failure Mode | Slow convergence | Divergence, $f'(x)=0$ |
| Computational Cost per Step | Low (only $f(c)$) | Higher ($f$ and $f'$) |
| Best Used When | Robustness required | Good initial guess available |
| KTU Board Frequency | Very High | Very High |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bisection Method — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Read inputs a, b, tolerance eps]
    B --> C[Evaluate f at a and f at b]
    C --> D{Is f a times f b less than zero?}
    D -- No --> E[Stop: No root in bracket]
    D -- Yes --> F[Compute midpoint c equals a plus b divided by two]
    F --> G[Evaluate f at c]
    G --> H{Is f at c equal to zero?}
    H -- Yes --> I([Root found: c])
    H -- No --> J{Is f a times f c less than zero?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Set b equals c]
    J -- No --> L[Set a equals c]
    K --> M{Is absolute b minus a less than eps?}
    L --> M
    M -- Yes --> N([Approximate root: c])
    M -- No --> F
    E --> Z([End])
    I --> Z
    N --> Z
```

### 4.2 Newton-Raphson Method — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start]) --> B[Read initial guess x0, tolerance eps]
    B --> C[Set x equals x0, set n equals 0]
    C --> D[Compute f at x and derivative f prime at x]
    D --> E{Is f prime at x equal to zero?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Stop: Method fails, derivative is zero]
    E -- No --> G[Update x new equals x minus f of x divided by f prime of x]
    G --> H[Compute f at x new]
    H --> I{Is absolute f at x new less than eps?}
    I -- Yes --> J([Root found: x new])
    I -- No --> K{Is absolute x new minus x less than eps?}
    K -- Yes --> J
    K -- No --> L[Set x equals x new, increment n]
    L --> M{n exceeds max iterations?}
    M -- Yes --> N([Stop: No convergence])
    M -- No --> D
    F --> Z([End])
    J --> Z
    N --> Z
```

### 4.3 Convergence Comparison — Visual Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Iteration 1] --> Q1[Bisection: Error 0.5]
    P1 --> Q2[Newton: Error 0.15]
    Q1 --> R1[Iteration 2: Error 0.25]
    Q2 --> R2[Iteration 2: Error 0.022]
    R1 --> S1[Iteration 3: Error 0.125]
    R2 --> S2[Iteration 3: Error 0.0004]
    S1 --> T1[Iteration 4: Error 0.0625]
    S2 --> T2[Iteration 4: Error 0.0000002]
    T2 --> U1([Newton: Converged])
    T1 --> V1[Bisection: still needs more iterations]
    V1 --> W1[Iteration 5: Error 0.03125]
    W1 --> W2[Iteration 6: Error 0.0156]
    W2 --> W3[Iteration 7: Error 0.0078]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Bisection vs Newton-Raphson

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BISECT[BISECTION SUBSYSTEM]
        direction TB
        B1[Input Interval a b] --> B2[Sign Change Check]
        B2 --> B3[Midpoint c]
        B3 --> B4[Bracket Selector]
        B4 --> B5[New Interval]
    end
    subgraph NEWTON[NEWTON RAPHSON SUBSYSTEM]
        direction TB
        N1[Input Initial Guess x0] --> N2[Derivative Evaluator]
        N2 --> N3[Tangent Line Intersect]
        N3 --> N4[Next Approximation]
        N4 --> N2
    end
    BISECT --> OUT([Output: Approximate Root])
    NEWTON --> OUT
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** Both subsystems accept different *input shapes* (interval vs single point) but produce the *same output structure* (an approximate root with quantified error). This is why KTU examiners often ask: "Why is Newton-Raphson faster despite needing a derivative?" — The answer is encoded in the *loopback* in the Newton block: **quadratic** vs **linear** contraction.
