In the notation adopted by the KTU 2024 Scheme: $$d(v) = \min_{(s \to \cdots \to v)} \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} w(v_i, v_{i+1})$$ where $w(u, v)$ denotes the weight of edge $(u, v)$ and the minimum is taken over **all valid paths** from source $s$ to destination $v$. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Spreading Ripple on Water" Model** > Imagine you drop a stone into a still pond at point $s$. Ripples spread outward at a speed inversely proportional to "terrain friction." The first ripple to reach every other point in the pond traces the **shortest (least-resistance) path** from $s$. Dijkstra's algorithm does exactly this: it treats the source as a stone and **iteratively expands** the frontier of "settled" vertices, always picking the closest unsettled neighbor next. A more familiar analogy: a **GPS navigation system**. When you set a start location, the GPS evaluates distances to every reachable intersection and iteratively locks in the shortest route segment by segment — this is Dijkstra's principle in action. ### 1.3 Key Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — High-Yield Properties** > - **Greedy Strategy:** At every iteration, the vertex with the smallest tentative distance is permanently **labelled (settled)**. > - **Non-Negative Edge Weights:** $w(u, v) \geq 0$ for all edges — this is the **mandatory precondition** for correctness. > - **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ using an array, or $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert)$ using a min-heap (priority queue). > - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. > - **Output:** Shortest distance from source $s$ to **every other vertex**, plus the predecessor tree for path reconstruction. ### 1.4 Why This Matters in Statistical & Computational Methods In PESFT865, Dijkstra's Method sits at the intersection of **optimization theory** and **graph-based computation**. It is widely used in: - Network flow analysis (transportation, telecommunications). - Bayesian network inference (shortest dependency chains). - Markov decision processes (optimal policy computation). - Cluster analysis and minimum spanning tree problems. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Underlying Greedy Principle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Greedy Choice Property (GCP):** Once a vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue with the **minimum** tentative distance, that distance is **final and optimal**. This is provable by contradiction — any alternative path to $u$ must pass through an unsettled vertex with distance $\geq d(u)$. ### 2.2 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition Let me break down the operational steps of Dijkstra's Method: - **Step 0 — Initialization:** - Set $d(s) = 0$ for the source vertex. - Set $d(v) = \infty$ for all $v \neq s$. - Initialize predecessor array $\pi(v) = \text{NIL}$ for all $v$. - Define the **unvisited set** $Q = V$. - **Step 1 — Selection (Greedy Extraction):** - Choose $u = \arg\min_{v \in Q} d(v)$ (the unvisited vertex with minimum tentative distance). - Remove $u$ from $Q$; mark $u$ as **visited/settled**. - **Step 2 — Relaxation (Edge Update):** - For each neighbor $v$ of $u$ still in $Q$: - Compute candidate distance: $\text{cand} = d(u) + w(u, v)$. - If $\text{cand} < d(v)$, then update $d(v) = \text{cand}$ and $\pi(v) = u$. - **Step 3 — Termination:** - Repeat Steps 1–2 until $Q = \emptyset$ or the target vertex is settled. ### 2.3 The Relaxation Operation — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Relaxation** of an edge $(u, v)$ is the test-and-update operation: > > $$\text{RELAX}(u, v, w): \quad d(v) \leftarrow \min\{d(v), \; d(u) + w(u, v)\}$$ > > This is the **heart** of Dijkstra's algorithm — every iteration performs one relaxation per outgoing edge of the currently settled vertex. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Term | Meaning | Mathematical Form | |---|---|---| | $G = (V, E)$ | Weighted graph | $V$ = vertex set, $E$ = edge set | | $w(u, v)$ | Edge weight (cost) | $w(u, v) \geq 0$ | | $d(v)$ | Tentative shortest distance from $s$ to $v$ | $d(v) \geq 0$ | | $\pi(v)$ | Predecessor of $v$ on shortest path | $\pi(v) \in V \cup \{\text{NIL}\}$ | | $Q$ | Min-priority queue of unvisited vertices | keyed by $d(v)$ | | Relaxation | Update test | $d(v) \leftarrow \min\{d(v), d(u) + w(u, v)\}$ | | Time Complexity | Array implementation | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ | | Time Complexity | Binary heap implementation | $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log\vert V \vert)$ | | Space Complexity | Storage requirement | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | | Termination | All nodes settled or $Q = \emptyset$ | after $\vert V \vert$ iterations | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** When writing the relaxation step in your answer script, **always show the comparison explicitly** (i.e., write both $d(v)_{\text{old}}$ and $d(v)_{\text{new}}$). Partial credit is awarded for visible logical structure. ### 2.5 Engineering Utility In real engineering practice, Dijkstra's Method underpins: - **Transportation engineering:** Shortest road/rail route planning. - **Telecommunications:** Minimum-hop and minimum-latency packet routing (OSPF protocol in IP networks). - **Robotics:** Path planning in known grid maps. - **Operations research:** Sub-routine for solving trans-shipment and minimum-cost flow problems. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example ### 3.1 Worked Example — Single-Source Shortest Path > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement (KTU-Style):** > Consider the directed weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edges: > > $E = \{(1,2,4), (1,3,2), (2,3,1), (2,4,5), (3,4,8), (3,5,10), (4,5,2)\}$ > > where each tuple is $(u, v, w)$ meaning an edge from $u$ to $v$ with weight $w$. Using **Dijkstra's Method**, find the shortest path from source vertex $s = 1$ to every other vertex. ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Execution (Fully Expanded) #### **Iteration 0 — Initialization** $$ \begin{aligned} d(1) &= 0, \quad d(2) = \infty, \quad d(3) = \infty, \quad d(4) = \infty, \quad d(5) = \infty \\ \pi(1) &= \text{NIL}, \quad \pi(2) = \pi(3) = \pi(4) = \pi(5) = \text{NIL} \\ Q &= \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Iteration 1 — Settle Vertex 1** The smallest tentative distance in $Q$ is $d(1) = 0$, so we **settle vertex 1**. Now **relax all outgoing edges from vertex 1:** $(1,2,4)$ and $(1,3,2)$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Relax}(1, 2, 4): \quad & d(2) = \min\{\infty, \; 0 + 4\} = 4, \quad \pi(2) = 1 \\ \text{Relax}(1, 3, 2): \quad & d(3) = \min\{\infty, \; 0 + 2\} = 2, \quad \pi(3) = 1 \end{aligned} $$ **Updated state:** - $d = (0, 4, 2, \infty, \infty)$ - Settled: $\{1\}$, Unvisited: $\{2, 3, 4, 5\}$ #### **Iteration 2 — Settle Vertex 3** The smallest tentative distance in $Q$ is $d(3) = 2$, so we **settle vertex 3**. Now **relax outgoing edges from vertex 3:** $(3,4,8)$ and $(3,5,10)$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Relax}(3, 4, 8): \quad & d(4) = \min\{\infty, \; 2 + 8\} = 10, \quad \pi(4) = 3 \\ \text{Relax}(3, 5, 10): \quad & d(5) = \min\{\infty, \; 2 + 10\} = 12, \quad \pi(5) = 3 \end{aligned} $$ **Updated state:** - $d = (0, 4, 2, 10, 12)$ - Settled: $\{1, 3\}$, Unvisited: $\{2, 4, 5\}$ #### **Iteration 3 — Settle Vertex 2** The smallest tentative distance in $Q$ is $d(2) = 4$, so we **settle vertex 2**. Now **relax outgoing edges from vertex 2:** $(2,3,1)$ and $(2,4,5)$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Relax}(2, 3, 1): \quad & d(3) = \min\{2, \; 4 + 1\} = 2 \quad (\text{no change — already settled}) \\ \text{Relax}(2, 4, 5): \quad & d(4) = \min\{10, \; 4 + 5\} = 9, \quad \pi(4) = 2 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Vertex 3:** Even though we relax edge $(2,3,1)$, we **do not update** $d(3)$ because vertex 3 is already settled (greedy optimality guarantee). This is a frequently-missed point in KTU valuation. **Updated state:** - $d = (0, 4, 2, 9, 12)$ - Settled: $\{1, 3, 2\}$, Unvisited: $\{4, 5\}$ #### **Iteration 4 — Settle Vertex 4** The smallest tentative distance in $Q$ is $d(4) = 9$, so we **settle vertex 4**. Now **relax outgoing edges from vertex 4:** $(4,5,2)$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Relax}(4, 5, 2): \quad & d(5) = \min\{12, \; 9 + 2\} = 11, \quad \pi(5) = 4 \end{aligned} $$ **Updated state:** - $d = (0, 4, 2, 9, 11)$ - Settled: $\{1, 3, 2, 4\}$, Unvisited: $\{5\}$ #### **Iteration 5 — Settle Vertex 5** The smallest tentative distance in $Q$ is $d(5) = 11$, so we **settle vertex 5**. No further relaxation needed. **Final State:** $Q = \emptyset$, algorithm terminates. ### 3.3 Final Result Compilation The shortest distances from source vertex 1 are: | Destination Vertex $v$ | Shortest Distance $d(v)$ | Predecessor $\pi(v)$ | Shortest Path | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 1 | **0** | NIL | $1$ | | 2 | **4** | 1 | $1 \to 2$ | | 3 | **2** | 1 | $1 \to 3$ | | 4 | **9** | 2 | $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4$ | | 5 | **11** | 4 | $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ | ### 3.4 Path Reconstruction (Backward Trace) To reconstruct the path to vertex 5: - Start at $v = 5$, $\pi(5) = 4$ → path so far: $(5, 4)$ - $\pi(4) = 2$ → path so far: $(5, 4, 2)$ - $\pi(2) = 1$ → path so far: $(5, 4, 2, 1)$ - $\pi(1) = \text{NIL}$ → **stop**, source reached. **Reverse the trace:** $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ — but wait, we use the predecessor chain $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ (since $\pi(2) = 1$ directly, the path through node 3 has lower cumulative weight via the relaxation). > [!NOTE] > **Verification by direct summation:** > > $$w(1,3) + w(3,2) + w(2,4) + w(4,5) = 2 + 1 + 5 + 2 = 10$$ > > $$\text{vs.} \quad w(1,2) + w(2,4) + w(4,5) = 4 + 5 + 2 = 11$$ > > The minimum-cost path $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 5$ has total cost **10**, confirming our result $d(5) = 11$ corresponds to the trace through predecessor updates at each iteration. ### 3.5 Derivation of the Greedy Optimality (Sketch) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Proof Sketch — Why Greedy Works for Non-Negative Weights:** > > Suppose when vertex $u$ is settled, its distance $d(u)$ is **not** optimal. Then there must exist another path $P$ from $s$ to $u$ with cost $c(P) < d(u)$. Consider the **first edge** along $P$ leaving the already-settled region. It enters some unvisited vertex $x$ with $d(x) \leq c(P) < d(u)$. But this contradicts the fact that $u$ was the minimum-cost unvisited vertex. Hence $d(u)$ is optimal. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Weighted Graph Under Analysis Below is the **block-level functional architecture flow** representing the graph used in the worked example, with edge weights annotated. ```mermaid graph TD node1["VERTEX 1
SOURCE d=0"] node2["VERTEX 2
d=4"] node3["VERTEX 3
d=2"] node4["VERTEX 4
d=9"] node5["VERTEX 5
d=11"] node1 -->|weight 4| node2 node1 -->|weight 2| node3 node3 -->|weight 1| node2 node2 -->|weight 5| node4 node3 -->|weight 8| node4 node3 -->|weight 10| node5 node4 -->|weight 2| node5 ``` ### 4.2 Dijkstra's Algorithm — Operational Flowchart The following block diagram illustrates the **high-level control flow** of Dijkstra's Method, from initialization through the main relax-update loop until termination. ```mermaid flowchart TD initA["START
Initialize d of source = 0"] initB["Initialize d of all other vertices = INFINITY"] initC["Initialize unvisited set Q = V"] initD["Initialize predecessor array pi = NIL"] loopStart{"Is Q empty?"} select["SELECT minimum distance vertex u from Q"] removeU["REMOVE u from Q
Mark u as settled"] checkNeigh{"For each neighbor v of u
still in Q"} computeCand["Compute candidate = d of u + w of u,v"] compareTest{"Is candidate less than d of v?"} updateD["UPDATE d of v = candidate
SET pi of v = u"] skipD["SKIP — no update"] doneOut["END
All shortest distances computed"] initA --> initB --> initC --> initD --> loopStart loopStart -->|NO| select --> removeU --> checkNeigh checkNeigh -->|YES| computeCand --> compareTest compareTest -->|YES| updateD --> loopStart compareTest -->|NO| skipD --> loopStart checkNeigh -->|NO| loopStart loopStart -->|YES| doneOut ``` ### 4.3 Iteration Trace — State Transitions This diagram shows how the **settled (visited) set grows** at each iteration in our worked example. ```mermaid flowchart LR iter1["ITERATION 1
Settled: 1
Shortest path: 1"] iter2["ITERATION 2
Settled: 1,3
Shortest paths: 1, 1->3"] iter3["ITERATION 3
Settled: 1,3,2
Shortest paths: ..., 1->3->2"] iter4["ITERATION 4
Settled: 1,3,2,4
Shortest paths: ..., 1->3->2->4"] iter5["ITERATION 5
Settled: 1,3,2,4,5
All paths complete"] iter1 -->|relax from 1| iter2 iter2 -->|relax from 3| iter3 iter3 -->|relax from 2| iter4 iter4 -->|relax from 4| iter5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** In each iteration, the vertex extracted from the priority queue is permanently fixed; subsequent iterations only update (relax) the neighbors of that fixed vertex. This **monotonic settlement** is the defining feature of Dijkstra's greedy strategy. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *State Dijkstra's algorithm. List any two limitations of the algorithm.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (2 Marks):** > Dijkstra's algorithm is a greedy graph-search technique used to find the **shortest path from a single source vertex** to all other vertices in a weighted graph with **non-negative edge weights**. > > **Limitations (1 Mark — any two):** > 1. It **fails for negative edge weights** — the greedy choice property does not hold. > 2. Its time complexity $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ is **inefficient for dense, large-scale graphs**. > 3. It solves only the **single-source** shortest path; for all-pairs, Floyd-Warshall is preferred. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *What is the "relaxation" operation in Dijkstra's algorithm? Write its mathematical form.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (2 Marks):** > Relaxation is the iterative process of testing whether going through a newly settled vertex $u$ provides a shorter route to its neighbor $v$, and updating $d(v)$ if so. > > **Mathematical Form (1 Mark):** > > $$d(v) = \min\{d(v), \; d(u) + w(u, v)\}$$ > > The predecessor is updated as $\pi(v) = u$ whenever the minimum is achieved through $u$. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern:** Module-level questions typically have sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Either option may be answered. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]** **(a)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the following directed graph with source vertex $A$. Show all iterations clearly. (7 Marks) Edges and weights: $(A, B, 4), \; (A, C, 2), \; (B, C, 3), \; (B, D, 2), \; (C, B, 1), \; (C, D, 4), \; (C, E, 5), \; (D, E, 1)$. **(b)** Reconstruct the shortest path from $A$ to $E$ and verify by direct weight summation. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) — Step-by-step Dijkstra Execution:** **Iteration 0 — Initialize:** $$ d(A) = 0, \; d(B) = d(C) = d(D) = d(E) = \infty, \; Q = \{A, B, C, D, E\} $$ **Iteration 1 — Settle A (d = 0):** Relax $(A,B,4)$: $d(B) = 0 + 4 = 4$, $\pi(B) = A$. Relax $(A,C,2)$: $d(C) = 0 + 2 = 2$, $\pi(C) = A$. **State:** $d = (A{:}0, B{:}4, C{:}2, D{:}\infty, E{:}\infty)$, Settled: $\{A\}$. **Iteration 2 — Settle C (d = 2):** Relax $(C,B,1)$: $d(B) = \min\{4, 2+1\} = 3$, $\pi(B) = C$. Relax $(C,D,4)$: $d(D) = \min\{\infty, 2+4\} = 6$, $\pi(D) = C$. Relax $(C,E,5)$: $d(E) = \min\{\infty, 2+5\} = 7$, $\pi(E) = C$. **State:** $d = (A{:}0, B{:}3, C{:}2, D{:}6, E{:}7)$, Settled: $\{A, C\}$. **Iteration 3 — Settle B (d = 3):** Relax $(B,C,3)$: $C$ already settled, no update. Relax $(B,D,2)$: $d(D) = \min\{6, 3+2\} = 5$, $\pi(D) = B$. **State:** $d = (A{:}0, B{:}3, C{:}2, D{:}5, E{:}7)$, Settled: $\{A, C, B\}$. **Iteration 4 — Settle D (d = 5):** Relax $(D,E,1)$: $d(E) = \min\{7, 5+1\} = 6$, $\pi(E) = D$. **State:** $d = (A{:}0, B{:}3, C{:}2, D{:}5, E{:}6)$, Settled: $\{A, C, B, D\}$. **Iteration 5 — Settle E (d = 6):** No further relaxation needed. Algorithm terminates. **(b) — Path Reconstruction and Verification:** Trace predecessors backward from $E$: - $\pi(E) = D$ - $\pi(D) = B$ - $\pi(B) = C$ - $\pi(C) = A$ - $\pi(A) = \text{NIL}$ — **stop**. **Shortest Path:** $A \to C \to B \to D \to E$ **Direct Weight Verification:** $$ w(A,C) + w(C,B) + w(B,D) + w(D,E) = 2 + 1 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{6} $$ This matches the computed $d(E) = 6$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the greedy choice property of Dijkstra's algorithm. Prove that once a vertex is settled, its distance is optimal. (7 Marks) **(b)** Apply Dijkstra's algorithm on the graph $G$ with vertices $\{P, Q, R, S, T\}$ and edges $(P,Q,7), (P,R,2), (R,Q,3), (Q,S,1), (R,S,5), (S,T,2), (R,T,6)$. Find shortest distances from $P$ to all vertices. (7 Marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) — Greedy Choice Property Proof:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Statement (2 Marks):** > The greedy choice property asserts that when vertex $u$ is extracted from the priority queue $Q$ with minimum tentative distance $d(u)$, the value $d(u)$ equals the true shortest-path distance from source $s$ to $u$. > > **Proof by Contradiction (5 Marks):** > Assume, for contradiction, that $d(u)$ is **not** optimal when $u$ is settled. Then there exists an alternative path $P: s \to \cdots \to u$ with total cost $c(P) < d(u)$. > > Consider the **first vertex $x$** on path $P$ that lies **outside the settled set** $S$ at the moment $u$ is selected. The vertex immediately before $x$ on $P$ is in $S$, say it is $y$. Since $y$ was settled before $u$, the relaxation step ensured: > > $$d(x) \leq d(y) + w(y, x) \leq c(P) < d(u)$$ > > But this means $x$ (which is unvisited) has a tentative distance **strictly less than** $d(u)$, contradicting the fact that $u$ was chosen as the minimum-cost unvisited vertex. Hence $d(u)$ must be optimal. $\blacksquare$ **(b) — Numerical Application:** **Iteration 0 — Initialize:** $$ d(P) = 0, \quad d(Q) = d(R) = d(S) = d(T) = \infty, \quad Q = \{P, Q, R, S, T\} $$ **Iteration 1 — Settle P (d = 0):** Relax $(P,Q,7)$: $d(Q) = 7$, $\pi(Q) = P$. Relax $(P,R,2)$: $d(R) = 2$, $\pi(R) = P$. **State:** $d = (P{:}0, Q{:}7, R{:}2, S{:}\infty, T{:}\infty)$, Settled: $\{P\}$. **Iteration 2 — Settle R (d = 2):** Relax $(R,Q,3)$: $d(Q) = \min\{7, 2+3\} = 5$, $\pi(Q) = R$. Relax $(R,S,5)$: $d(S) = \min\{\infty, 2+5\} = 7$, $\pi(S) = R$. Relax $(R,T,6)$: $d(T) = \min\{\infty, 2+6\} = 8$, $\pi(T) = R$. **State:** $d = (P{:}0, Q{:}5, R{:}2, S{:}7, T{:}8)$, Settled: $\{P, R\}$. **Iteration 3 — Settle Q (d = 5):** Relax $(Q,S,1)$: $d(S) = \min\{7, 5+1\} = 6$, $\pi(S) = Q$. **State:** $d = (P{:}0, Q{:}5, R{:}2, S{:}6, T{:}8)$, Settled: $\{P, R, Q\}$. **Iteration 4 — Settle S (d = 6):** Relax $(S,T,2)$: $d(T) = \min\{8, 6+2\} = 8$ (no improvement, tie). **State:** $d = (P{:}0, Q{:}5, R{:}2, S{:}6, T{:}8)$, Settled: $\{P, R, Q, S\}$. **Iteration 5 — Settle T (d = 8):** No further updates. Algorithm terminates. **Final Shortest Distances from P:** | Destination | Shortest Distance | Path | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | P | 0 | $P$ | | Q | 5 | $P \to R \to Q$ | | R | 2 | $P \to R$ | | S | 6 | $P \to R \to Q \to S$ | | T | 8 | $P \to R \to T$ | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting to update predecessors ($\pi$ array).** Students often update only $d(v)$ and forget to record $\pi(v)$, losing 1–2 marks in path reconstruction sub-parts. > 2. **Applying relaxation on already-settled vertices.** This is logically redundant — explicitly state "vertex is already settled, no update." > 3. **Confusing directed vs undirected graphs.** In undirected graphs, both $(u,v)$ and $(v,u)$ carry the same weight; in directed graphs, only the listed direction exists. KTU examiners **deduct marks** if edges are treated inconsistently. > 4. **Not showing both candidates in the comparison.** Always write $d(v)_{\text{new}} = \min\{d(v)_{\text{old}}, d(u) + w(u,v)\}$ with values substituted — partial credit depends on visible work. > 5. **Writing the algorithm in pseudo-code instead of iteration tables.** KTU 2024 scheme prefers **tabular iteration logs** showing $d(v)$, $\pi(v)$, and settled set per iteration. > 6. **Ignoring the non-negative edge weight constraint.** Dijkstra's algorithm is **invalid** for negative weights — mention this caveat in any "limitations" or "applications" question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for Dijkstra's Method:** - **Core Purpose:** Finds shortest path from a **single source** to all other vertices in a graph with **non-negative** edge weights. - **Algorithm Class:** Greedy, graph-search, single-source shortest path (SSSP). - **Initialization Rule:** Source distance = **0**; all other distances = **$\infty$**. - **Settlement Rule:** Always pick the unvisited vertex with the **smallest tentative distance** — its distance is now final. - **Relaxation Rule:** For each neighbor $v$ of the settled vertex $u$, update $d(v) = \min\{d(v), d(u) + w(u,v)\}$ and set $\pi(v) = u$ if improved. - **Termination:** When all vertices are settled, or when target vertex is settled (for single-target queries). - **Predecessor Tracking:** The $\pi$ array stores the parent in the shortest-path tree — essential for **path reconstruction**. - **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ with array; $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)\log\vert V \vert)$ with binary heap. - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. - **Limitation 1:** Fails for **negative edge weights** (use Bellman-Ford instead). - **Limitation 2:** Inefficient for **all-pairs** shortest path (use Floyd-Warshall instead). - **Engineering Applications:** GPS navigation, OSPF routing in IP networks, robotic path planning, network flow optimization, Markov decision processes. - **Comparison with BFS:** BFS finds shortest path in **unweighted** graphs; Dijkstra generalizes this to **non-negative weighted** graphs. - **Comparison with Bellman-Ford:** Bellman-Ford handles negative weights but is slower ($\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$); Dijkstra is faster but restricted to non-negative weights. - **KTU 2024 Board Expectation:** Always present solutions in **iteration-table format** with explicit columns for current vertex, tentative distances, predecessor updates, and the settled set.

topics.title: Decision Under Certainty
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT865 - STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL METHODS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decision Under Certainty

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Decision Under Certainty** is a category of decision-making in Operations Research (OR) and Management Science wherein the decision-maker has **complete, perfect, and unambiguous knowledge** of:
- All available courses of action (alternatives / decision variables),
- All states of nature (environmental parameters), and
- The exact payoff, cost, or outcome associated with each alternative.

Mathematically, if $A = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$ is the set of alternatives and the outcome $V(a_i)$ is a **deterministic scalar** (not a probability distribution), then the problem reduces to a pure optimization:

$$
a^* = \arg\!\max_{a_i \in A} V(a_i) \quad \text{(profit maximization)}
$$

$$
a^* = \arg\!\min_{a_i \in A} C(a_i) \quad \text{(cost minimization)}
$$

In contrast to *Decision Under Risk* (probabilistic outcomes) and *Decision Under Uncertainty* (no probabilities, only possible states), the certainty environment **eliminates probabilistic reasoning** entirely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PESFT865 – Module 3):**
> Decision under certainty forms the foundation for nearly every deterministic OR model — Linear Programming, Transportation, Assignment, PERT/CPM, Sequencing, Queuing (M/D/1), and Replacement models. Mastery of this classification is **mandatory** before progressing to stochastic models in Module 4.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The Morning Commute Decision:**

Imagine you live in Trivandrum and must reach the KTU main campus at 8:30 AM. You check Google Maps the night before and see the route is **always** 14 km and takes exactly 35 minutes on the bypass road. There is **no rain forecast**, **no traffic jam risk**, and **no accident probability**. You simply choose the *shortest path* with full confidence.

- You know the alternatives: bypass road, coastal road, or local route.
- You know the exact travel time for each.
- You **do not** flip a coin or assign probabilities — you just pick the optimal one.

That is **Decision Under Certainty**: deterministic inputs, deterministic outputs, optimal choice = best evaluated alternative.

Another simple analogy is a **grocery bill calculation** — you know the price of every item and your budget; you simply select items to maximize utility subject to a known constraint.

---

## 1.3 Key Characteristics of Decision Under Certainty

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinctive Features (Board-Exam Favourite):**

1. **Perfect Information:** Every parameter is a known constant — no random variables.
2. **Single Outcome per Alternative:** Each action maps to exactly one payoff/cost.
3. **Deterministic Model:** Solved using classical optimization (LP, calculus, graph theory).
4. **No Expected Value Required:** Since probabilities are absent, $\mathbb{E}[X] = X$ trivially.
5. **Criterion Simplifies to:** Maximize $V(a_i)$ or Minimize $C(a_i)$ — a single objective.
6. **Solution Exists and is Unique** (under convexity/concavity assumptions).
7. **Solvable by OR Techniques:** LP, Transportation, Assignment, Sequencing, Replacement, EOQ, PERT/CPM, Queuing (Deterministic), Break-Even Analysis.

## 1.4 Classification of Decision Environments (KTU Reference)

$$
\text{Decision Environments} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Certainty} & \rightarrow \text{Outcomes known with certainty} \\
\text{Risk} & \rightarrow \text{Probabilities of states are known} \\
\text{Uncertainty} & \rightarrow \text{States exist, probabilities unknown} \\
\text{Conflict} & \rightarrow \text{Multiple competitive decision-makers (Game Theory)}
\end{cases}
$$

This taxonomy is a **high-yield KTU question** — expect a 3-mark or short-note variant almost every exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Decision Under Certainty can be formally modeled as a **mathematical programming problem**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Optimize} \quad & Z = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & g_j(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \leq b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m \\
& x_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $x_i$ = decision variables (controllable inputs).
- $b_j$ = parameters (constants, completely known).
- $f(\cdot)$ = deterministic objective function (profit/cost/time).
- $g_j(\cdot)$ = deterministic constraints.

**Why this works:** Because every coefficient in $f$ and $g_j$ is a fixed number (not a random variable), the feasible region $S$ is a closed, well-defined polyhedron (for LP) or convex set (for NLP). Optimality conditions (KKT, Simplex, Branch & Bound, Hungarian Method) apply deterministically.

---

## 2.2 Major OR Models Classified as "Decision Under Certainty"

| # | Model | Typical Objective | Deterministic Inputs |
|---|-------|-------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Linear Programming (LP)** | Max profit / Min cost | $a_{ij}, b_i, c_j$ all known |
| 2 | **Transportation Problem** | Min shipping cost | Supply, demand, cost matrix |
| 3 | **Assignment Problem** | Min assignment cost / Max efficiency | Cost/efficiency matrix |
| 4 | **Sequencing Problem** | Min makespan / Idle time | Processing times |
| 5 | **Replacement Models** | Min avg. cost / Max efficiency | Item cost, salvage, MTTF |
| 6 | **Inventory (EOQ)** | Min total cost | Demand rate $D$, ordering cost $C_o$, holding cost $C_h$ |
| 7 | **PERT/CPM** | Min project duration | Activity durations (deterministic in CPM) |
| 8 | **Queuing (M/D/1, D/D/1)** | Min waiting cost | Arrival/service rates |
| 9 | **Break-Even Analysis** | Find profit-zero volume | Fixed cost, variable cost, price |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield — memorize verbatim.**

### 2.3.1 Break-Even Analysis (BE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue (TR)} &= p \cdot Q \\
\text{Total Cost (TC)} &= F + v \cdot Q \\
\text{Break-Even Quantity } Q^* &= \frac{F}{p - v} \\
\text{Break-Even Revenue } R^* &= \frac{F}{1 - \tfrac{v}{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $F$ = Fixed Cost, $v$ = Variable cost per unit, $p$ = Selling price per unit.

### 2.3.2 Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) — Wilson's Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} \\
\text{Total Minimum Cost } TC^* &= \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} \\
\text{Number of Orders/Year } N^* &= \frac{D}{Q^*} = \sqrt{\frac{D C_h}{2 C_o}} \\
\text{Time Between Orders } T^* &= \frac{Q^*}{D}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $D$ = annual demand (units/yr), $C_o$ = ordering cost per order (₹), $C_h$ = holding cost per unit per year (₹).

### 2.3.3 Linear Programming (Standard Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Max } Z &= c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2 + \cdots + c_n x_n \\
\text{s.t. } & a_{i1} x_1 + a_{i2} x_2 + \cdots + a_{in} x_n \leq b_i, \quad i=1,\ldots,m \\
& x_j \geq 0, \quad j=1,\ldots,n
\end{aligned}
$$

Solved by **Simplex Method** (Big-M or Two-Phase for infeasible starts).

### 2.3.4 PERT/CPM (Critical Path — Deterministic CPM)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ES}_j &= \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} (\text{EF}_i) \\
\text{EF}_j &= \text{ES}_j + t_j \\
\text{LS}_j &= \min_{k \in \text{succ}(j)} (\text{LF}_k) - t_j \\
\text{LF}_j &= \text{LS}_{\text{next}} \\
\text{Slack}_j &= \text{LS}_j - \text{ES}_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Critical Path = path where Slack = 0 (all activities).

### 2.3.5 Replacement Model — Group Replacement

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average Cost per Period } &= \frac{\text{Total Cost in Cycle}}{\text{Length of Cycle}} \\
\text{Optimal Cycle Length } n^* &= \min_n \left[ \frac{C(n) + S(n)}{n} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $C(n)$ = cumulative cost, $S(n)$ = scrap/salvage adjustment.

### 2.3.6 Sequencing — $n$ Jobs on 2 Machines

$$
\text{Min Makespan} = \max\left( \sum p_{1j}, \, \sum p_{2j} \right) + \min_{p_{1j}} p_{1j} + \sum_{j=1}^{n} \min(p_{1j}, p_{2j})
$$

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Decision Under Certainty Application |
|--------------------|--------------------------------------|
| **Manufacturing** | EOQ for raw materials, LP for production mix |
| **Construction** | CPM for project scheduling, resource leveling |
| **Logistics** | Transportation & assignment for fleet routing |
| **Maintenance** | Replacement models for machine overhauls |
| **Telecom** | Queuing for deterministic call arrivals |
| **Finance** | Break-even & CVP analysis for product launch |
| **Software Projects** | PERT for sprint planning (deterministic tasks) |

## 2.5 Steps in Decision Under Certainty (Algorithm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Solution Procedure (KTU 2-Mark Question):**

1. **Identify** the decision variables (what we control).
2. **Formulate** the objective function (profit / cost / time).
3. **List** all deterministic constraints (resources, demand, capacity).
4. **Select** the appropriate OR technique (LP, TP, AP, EOQ, etc.).
5. **Solve** using the deterministic algorithm (Simplex, Hungarian, calculus).
6. **Validate** the solution against all constraints.
7. **Implement** and monitor (sensitivity analysis if coefficients vary slightly).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Break-Even Point

The **Break-Even Point (BEP)** is the production quantity where Total Revenue equals Total Cost. At this point, profit is zero — neither profit nor loss.

**Step 1 — Define the variables:**

Let:
- $F$ = Total fixed cost (₹/period) — rent, salary, depreciation.
- $v$ = Variable cost per unit (₹/unit) — raw material, direct labor.
- $p$ = Selling price per unit (₹/unit).
- $Q$ = Quantity produced and sold (units).
- $Z(Q)$ = Net profit as a function of $Q$ (₹).

**Step 2 — Write the cost and revenue equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost: } TC(Q) &= F + v Q \\
\text{Total Revenue: } TR(Q) &= p \cdot Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express profit as the difference:**

$$
Z(Q) = TR(Q) - TC(Q) = p Q - (F + v Q) = (p - v) Q - F
$$

The term $(p - v)$ is called the **Contribution Margin per unit** (denoted $CM$).

**Step 4 — Set profit to zero to find BEP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z(Q) &= 0 \\
(p - v) Q - F &= 0 \\
(p - v) Q &= F \\
\boxed{Q^* &= \frac{F}{p - v}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive Break-Even Revenue (optional):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^* &= p \cdot Q^* = p \cdot \frac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \frac{p \cdot F}{p(1 - \tfrac{v}{p})} = \frac{F}{1 - \tfrac{v}{p}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation of margin of safety:**

$$
\text{Margin of Safety} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BE Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100\%
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the EOQ Formula (Wilson's Formula)

EOQ minimizes the **Total Inventory Cost** = Ordering Cost + Holding Cost.

**Step 1 — Define parameters:**

- $D$ = Annual demand (units/yr).
- $Q$ = Order quantity per order (units).
- $C_o$ = Ordering cost per order (₹/order).
- $C_h$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹/unit/yr).
- Average inventory (assuming instantaneous replenishment) $= \tfrac{Q}{2}$.

**Step 2 — Write the two cost components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Annual Ordering Cost } OC(Q) &= \frac{D}{Q} \cdot C_o \\
\text{Annual Holding Cost } HC(Q) &= \frac{Q}{2} \cdot C_h
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total annual cost:**

$$
TC(Q) = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q} + \frac{C_h \cdot Q}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate w.r.t. $Q$ and set to zero:**

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $Q$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{C_h}{2} &= \frac{D \cdot C_o}{Q^2} \\
Q^2 &= \frac{2 D \cdot C_o}{C_h} \\
\boxed{Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Confirm second derivative is positive (minimum):**

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D \cdot C_o}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \text{for all } Q > 0
$$

Hence $Q^*$ is a **global minimum** — convex objective function.

**Step 7 — Substitute back to get minimum total cost:**

$$
TC^* = \frac{D \cdot C_o}{\sqrt{\tfrac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}} + \frac{C_h}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}} = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful Property (KTU 2-Mark):** At $Q^*$, the **ordering cost equals the holding cost**:
> $\tfrac{D C_o}{Q^*} = \tfrac{C_h Q^*}{2}$. This is the **equating property** — frequently tested.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Break-Even Analysis

**Problem (KTU 2024 Typical):**
A company manufactures a product with fixed cost ₹50,000, variable cost ₹120 per unit, and selling price ₹200 per unit. Find:
(a) Break-even quantity,
(b) Break-even revenue,
(c) Profit at $Q = 1000$ units.

**Solution:**

**(a) Break-Even Quantity:**

$$
Q^* = \frac{F}{p - v} = \frac{50000}{200 - 120} = \frac{50000}{80} = 625 \text{ units}
$$

**(b) Break-Even Revenue:**

$$
R^* = p \cdot Q^* = 200 \times 625 = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000
$$

**Verification using alternate formula:**

$$
R^* = \frac{F}{1 - \tfrac{v}{p}} = \frac{50000}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{50000}{0.4} = \text{₹ } 1{,}25{,}000 \quad \checkmark
$$

**(c) Profit at $Q = 1000$:**

$$
Z(1000) = (p - v) Q - F = (200 - 120)(1000) - 50000 = 80000 - 50000 = \text{₹ } 30000
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — EOQ

**Problem:**
Annual demand for a component is $D = 10{,}000$ units. Ordering cost is ₹500 per order. Holding cost is ₹20 per unit per year. Find EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders, and minimum total cost.

**Solution:**

**(a) EOQ:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 500}{20}} = \sqrt{\frac{10^7}{20}} = \sqrt{500000} \approx 707.11 \text{ units}
$$

**(b) Number of orders per year:**

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{10000}{707.11} \approx 14.14 \text{ orders/year}
$$

**(c) Time between orders (assuming 365 working days):**

$$
T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{707.11}{10000} \text{ yr} = 0.0707 \text{ yr} \approx 25.8 \text{ days}
$$

**(d) Minimum total cost:**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 10000 \cdot 500 \cdot 20} = \sqrt{2 \times 10^8} = \sqrt{200000000} \approx \text{₹ } 14142.14
$$

**(e) Verification of equating property:**

$$
\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{10000}{707.11} \times 500 \approx 7071.07
$$

$$
\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{707.11}{2} \times 20 \approx 7071.07 \quad \checkmark
$$

Both costs are equal — confirms optimum.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Decision Environments

| Aspect | Certainty | Risk | Uncertainty | Conflict |
|--------|-----------|------|-------------|----------|
| **State probabilities** | Not needed (only one state) | Known | Unknown | Determined by opponents |
| **Outcome type** | Deterministic scalar | Random variable $\mathbb{E}[X]$ | Range of payoffs | Payoff matrix |
| **Decision rule** | Optimize $f(x)$ | EMV / EOL / Utility | Maximin / Maximax / Hurwicz / Laplace / Savage | Saddle / Dominance / Nash |
| **Tool** | LP, TP, AP, EOQ, CPM, Sequencing | Decision tree, Bayes' Theorem | Decision matrix criteria | Game Theory |
| **Information** | Perfect & complete | Probabilistic | Incomplete | Strategic interaction |
| **Example** | Daily routine, EOQ | Insurance investment | New product launch | Chess, pricing war |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**"Distinguish between Decision Making under Certainty, Risk, and Uncertainty. Give one example for each."**

**Model Answer:**

| Decision Type | Information Available | Decision Criterion | Example |
|---------------|----------------------|---------------------|---------|
| **Certainty** | All parameters known with 100% accuracy | Optimize the deterministic objective | Deciding EOQ when $D, C_o, C_h$ are fixed |
| **Risk** | Probabilities of outcomes are known | Maximize Expected Monetary Value (EMV) or minimize Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL) | Investing in a stock whose return distribution is known |
| **Uncertainty** | Outcomes known, but probabilities are unknown | Maximin / Maximax / Hurwicz / Laplace / Savage | Launching a product in a new market with no historical data |

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- 1 mark for each correct distinction + example.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**"State the assumptions of the Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) model."**

**Model Answer:**

The classical EOQ model rests on the following deterministic assumptions:

1. **Known and constant demand rate** $D$ (units per unit time, no variation).
2. **Instantaneous replenishment** — order arrives all at once (infinite production rate).
3. **Constant lead time** — fixed, known time between placing and receiving an order.
4. **No stockouts permitted** — inventory never goes negative.
5. **Ordering cost** $C_o$ is fixed per order, independent of $Q$.
6. **Holding cost** $C_h$ is linear in average inventory (i.e., proportional to $Q/2$).
7. **Single item, infinite planning horizon** — only one SKU is considered.
8. **No quantity discounts** — unit purchase price is constant.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Any 6 assumptions — 0.5 mark each.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

#### (a) Derive the EOQ formula from first principles. State clearly the equating property. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define variables (1 mark):**
- $D$ = annual demand (units/year)
- $Q$ = order quantity per order
- $C_o$ = ordering cost per order (₹/order)
- $C_h$ = holding/carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year)

**Step 2 — Express total cost function (1 mark):**

$$
TC(Q) = \underbrace{\frac{D}{Q} C_o}_{\text{Annual Ordering Cost}} + \underbrace{\frac{Q}{2} C_h}_{\text{Annual Holding Cost}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply first-order optimality (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{d(TC)}{dQ} = -\frac{D C_o}{Q^2} + \frac{C_h}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically (1 mark):**

$$
Q^2 = \frac{2 D C_o}{C_h} \implies Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify second-order condition (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{d^2(TC)}{dQ^2} = \frac{2 D C_o}{Q^3} > 0 \quad \forall Q > 0
$$

Hence $Q^*$ is a **global minimum**.

**Step 6 — State the Equating Property (1 mark):**

At the optimum $Q^*$, the ordering cost exactly equals the holding cost:

$$
\frac{D C_o}{Q^*} = \frac{C_h Q^*}{2}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back for minimum total cost (1 mark):**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}
$$

---

#### (b) A factory uses 20,000 units/year of a component. Ordering cost is ₹400/order. Holding cost is ₹10/unit/year. Calculate EOQ, number of orders/year, optimal interval between orders, and minimum total inventory cost. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
$D = 20{,}000$ units/yr, $C_o = \text{₹} 400$, $C_h = \text{₹} 10$/unit/yr.

**Solution:**

**(a) EOQ:**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20000 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{10}} = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^6} = 1264.91 \text{ units}
$$

**(b) Number of orders per year:**

$$
N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{20000}{1264.91} \approx 15.81 \text{ orders/yr}
$$

**(c) Optimal interval (assume 250 working days/year):**

$$
T^* = \frac{250}{N^*} = \frac{250}{15.81} \approx 15.81 \text{ days}
$$

**(d) Minimum total cost:**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 \times 20000 \times 400 \times 10} = \sqrt{1.6 \times 10^8} = \text{₹ } 12{,}649.11
$$

**Verification of equating property:**

$$
\text{Ordering Cost} = \frac{20000}{1264.91} \times 400 = \text{₹ } 6324.55
$$

$$
\text{Holding Cost} = \frac{1264.91}{2} \times 10 = \text{₹ } 6324.55 \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

#### (a) Explain with suitable diagrams the Break-Even Analysis. Derive the formulas for Break-Even Quantity and Break-Even Revenue. **(7 marks)**

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
Break-Even Analysis (BEA), also called **Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis**, is the technique of finding the sales volume at which Total Revenue equals Total Cost, i.e., profit = 0.

**Step 2 — Equations (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Cost: } TC &= F + v Q \\
\text{Total Revenue: } TR &= p Q \\
\text{Profit: } Z &= TR - TC = (p - v)Q - F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Diagram (description) (2 marks):**

```
  ₹         TR line (slope = p)
  │           /
  │          /  
  │         /        TC line (slope = v, intercept = F)
  │        /       /
  │       /      /
  │      /     /
  │     /    /
  │    /   /  ← Loss region (TC > TR)
  │   /  /
  │  /./  ← Break-Even Point (Q*, R*)
  │ / /
  │//   ← Profit region (TR > TC)
  └──────────────── Q (units)
       Q*
```

- The TR line (origin, slope $p$) intersects TC line (slope $v$, $y$-intercept $F$) at the BEP.
- Below $Q^*$: Loss (TC > TR).
- Above $Q^*$: Profit (TR > TC).

**Step 4 — Derive BEP Quantity (1 mark):**

$$
(p - v)Q^* = F \implies \boxed{Q^* = \frac{F}{p - v}}
$$

**Step 5 — Derive BEP Revenue (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{R^* = \frac{F}{1 - \tfrac{v}{p}}}
$$

---

#### (b) A company has fixed cost ₹80,000, variable cost ₹150/unit, and selling price ₹250/unit. Compute: **(i)** Break-Even Point in units, **(ii)** Break-Even Sales Revenue, **(iii)** Profit when sales volume = 1500 units, **(iv)** Margin of Safety if actual sales = ₹4,00,000. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
$F = 80000$, $v = 150$, $p = 250$.

**Solution:**

**(i) Break-Even Quantity:**

$$
Q^* = \frac{80000}{250 - 150} = \frac{80000}{100} = 800 \text{ units}
$$

**(ii) Break-Even Sales Revenue:**

$$
R^* = 800 \times 250 = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
$$

**(iii) Profit at $Q = 1500$:**

$$
Z(1500) = (p - v)Q - F = (250 - 150)(1500) - 80000 = 100 \times 1500 - 80000 = \text{₹ } 70000
$$

**(iv) Margin of Safety:**

$$
\text{Margin of Safety \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BE Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
= \frac{400000 - 200000}{400000} \times 100\% = 50\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in BEP formula** — students often write $Q^* = \tfrac{F}{p}$ instead of $\tfrac{F}{p-v}$. Result becomes wrong by orders of magnitude.
> 2. **Margin of Safety confusion** — it is expressed either in **rupees** (absolute) or in **percentage** (relative). Mention which one you are computing.
> 3. **In EOQ problems, do not forget the equating property verification** — it is a 1-mark "free gift" you MUST claim.
> 4. **In Break-Even, confuse between BEP in units vs. BEP in revenue** — the question will explicitly state. Read twice.
> 5. **Use consistent units** — if demand is monthly, holding cost must also be monthly; convert annual values carefully.
> 6. **In Decision-making classification** — do not mix "Certainty" with "Risk". Certainty = one outcome, deterministic. Risk = multiple outcomes WITH probabilities.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Decision Under Certainty:**

- **Definition:** Decision problem where every parameter (alternative, state, payoff) is **known and deterministic**.
- **No probabilities** — expected value = the value itself.
- **Solution rule:** Simply **maximize profit** or **minimize cost/time** among the available alternatives.
- **Classified under certainty:** LP, Transportation, Assignment, Sequencing, CPM (deterministic), EOQ, Replacement models, Queuing (M/D/1, D/D/1), Break-Even Analysis.
- **NOT classified under certainty:** PERT (probabilistic durations), Decision Trees, Bayesian Models, Game Theory, Maximin/Maximax/Hurwicz.
- **Break-Even Formulae:**

$$
Q^* = \frac{F}{p - v}, \qquad R^* = \frac{F}{1 - \tfrac{v}{p}}
$$

- **Margin of Safety %:**

$$
\text{MoS \%} = \frac{\text{Actual Sales} - \text{BE Sales}}{\text{Actual Sales}} \times 100
$$

- **EOQ Formula (Wilson's):**

$$
Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 D C_o}{C_h}}
$$

- **Minimum Total Cost:**

$$
TC^* = \sqrt{2 D C_o C_h}
$$

- **Equating Property:** At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost — always verify in the exam.
- **Number of orders:** $N^* = D/Q^*$.
- **Time between orders:** $T^* = Q^*/D$ (in same time units as $D$).
- **CPM Forward Pass:** $\text{ES}_j = \max_{i \in \text{pred}(j)} \text{EF}_i$.
- **CPM Backward Pass:** $\text{LF}_j = \min_{k \in \text{succ}(j)} \text{LS}_k$.
- **Slack:** $\text{LF}_j - \text{EF}_j$ (or $\text{LS}_j - \text{ES}_j$).
- **Critical Path:** Path with zero total slack.
- **Assumptions of EOQ:** Constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no stockouts, fixed $C_o$ and $C_h$, single item, no quantity discount, infinite horizon.
- **Assumptions of Break-Even:** Linear cost & revenue functions, constant $F$, $v$, $p$ over relevant range, single product.
- **Contribution Margin:** $CM = p - v$ — appears in every BEP computation.
- **KTU favourite 2-mark questions:**
  - Define Decision Under Certainty with an example.
  - Differentiate between certainty, risk, and uncertainty.
  - List assumptions of EOQ model.
  - State the equating property of EOQ.
  - Define Break-Even Point and Margin of Safety.
- **KTU favourite 7-mark questions:**
  - Derive EOQ formula + numerical.
  - Solve BEP problem with multiple sub-parts.
  - Formulate LP problem and solve by Simplex method.
  - Solve Transportation / Assignment Problem using a numerical cost matrix.
- **Always show units** in final numerical answers — partial marks are often given for correct dimensional consistency.
- **Use a sketch/diagram** whenever the question asks for one — it usually carries 1–2 marks and is the easiest mark to score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Decision Tree** is a graphical, chronological, and mathematical model used to represent a sequence of interrelated decisions under conditions of **risk** (where outcomes are probabilistic) and **uncertainty** (where outcomes are indeterminate or partially unknown). It maps an initial decision point through chance events to a set of terminal payoffs, allowing the decision-maker to compute the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** at each node and select the alternative with the optimal value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Decision trees fall under the domain of *Decision Analysis and Bayesian Inference*. They are explicitly mapped to **Course Outcome CO3**: *Apply probabilistic and statistical models to solve decision-making problems under risk and uncertainty.*

### 1.2 Three Structural Components of a Decision Tree

| Symbol | Node Type | Role |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Square (■)** | Decision Node | Point of choice among alternatives — controlled by the decision-maker |
| **Circle (●)** | Chance Node | Point of probabilistic events — controlled by nature/environment |
| **Triangle (▲)** | Outcome Node / Terminal | Final payoff or consequence — end of a branch |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Weather and the Picnic":**
> Imagine you are deciding whether to **host an outdoor picnic** tomorrow. This is a *decision node*. Once you decide to proceed, nature steps in: there is a **70% chance of sunshine** and a **30% chance of rain**. These are *chance nodes*. If it rains, your picnic costs ₹15,000; if it is sunny, you earn ₹30,000 in ticket sales. A decision tree is simply a structured way of "drawing out" this sequence of choices and outcomes, and then calculating the *expected gain* so you can decide intelligently rather than emotionally.

- **Risk**: Probabilities of outcomes are *known* (objective or subjective) — e.g., 70% rain, 30% shine.
- **Uncertainty**: Probabilities of outcomes are *unknown* — only the *magnitude* of possible outcomes is known. We use criteria like **Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, or Minimax Regret**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric:** The expected value of any branch is computed by multiplying each outcome by its probability and summing the products. The chosen alternative must **maximize EMV** (for profit) or **minimize EOL** (for loss).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Decision Tree Construction

The construction of a decision tree follows a strict **5-step algorithmic logic**:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Decision:** Define the main alternative the decision-maker faces. This becomes the root square node.
- **Step 2 — Draw Decision Branches:** From the root, draw one branch per available alternative. Each branch represents a *controllable action*.
- **Step 3 — Attach Chance Nodes:** At the tip of each decision branch, place a circular chance node for every probabilistic event that may follow.
- **Step 4 — Label Probabilities and Payoffs:** Assign probabilities $p_i$ to chance branches and monetary payoffs $V_i$ to terminal nodes. Ensure **$\sum p_i = 1$** at each chance node.
- **Step 5 — Roll-Back (Fold-Back) Analysis:** Compute the EMV at every chance node, propagate it back to the preceding decision node, and **prune** (cross out) suboptimal branches.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | $\text{EMV}_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot V_{ij}$ | Expected Monetary Value of alternative $j$ | Roll-back at chance nodes |
| 2 | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EMV}_{\text{with PI}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{without PI}}$ | Expected Value of Perfect Information | Maximum amount to pay for a survey/expert report |
| 3 | $\text{EOL}_j = \sum_{i} p_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | Expected Opportunity Loss of alternative $j$ | Loss-based decision criterion |
| 4 | $P(A_i \mid B) = \dfrac{P(B \mid A_i) \cdot P(A_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid A_j) \cdot P(A_j)}$ | Bayes' Theorem (posterior probability) | Updating probabilities after sample information |
| 5 | $\text{EMV}_{\text{sample info}} = \sum_{j} P(R_j) \cdot (\text{Best payoff given } R_j)$ | Expected Value with Sample Information (EVwSI) | Decision trees with sample/test information |
| 6 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{EMV}_{\text{sample}} - \text{EMV}_{\text{prior}}}{\text{EVPI}}$ | Efficiency of Sample Information | $0 \le \alpha \le 1$ |
| 7 | $U(x) = a + b \cdot \ln(x)$ / $\sqrt{x}$ / $(x - c)$ | Utility Functions for risk-averse / risk-neutral / risk-seekers | Utility-based decision making |
| 8 | $\text{Maximax} = \max_j \max_i V_{ij}$ | Optimistic Criterion | Maximize the best possible payoff |
| 9 | $\text{Maximin} = \max_j \min_i V_{ij}$ | Pessimistic Criterion | Maximize the worst-case payoff |
| 10 | $\text{Minimax Regret} = \min_j \max_i R_{ij}$ where $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{ik} - V_{ij}$ | Regret / Opportunity Loss Criterion | Minimize the maximum opportunity loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** For any chance node, the sum of probabilities of outgoing branches **must equal 1** ($\sum p_i = 1$). Violation of this rule is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Optimal bidding strategy for construction tenders under geological risk.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** "Make-or-Buy" decisions for components with variable demand.
- **Industrial Engineering:** Equipment replacement policies under stochastic failure rates.
- **Software Engineering:** Algorithm selection under varying input distributions.
- **Economics & Finance:** Portfolio selection under market volatility.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Roll-Back

Consider a decision alternative $A_j$ leading to $n$ possible states of nature $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$, each with probability $p_i$ and monetary payoff $V_{ij}$.

$$
\text{EMV}_j \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot V_{ij}
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_j \;=\; p_1 V_{1j} + p_2 V_{2j} + p_3 V_{3j} + \dots + p_n V_{nj}
$$

**Logic:** Each payoff $V_{ij}$ is weighted by its likelihood $p_i$. Summing over all mutually exclusive and exhaustive states yields the long-run average gain per trial if alternative $A_j$ is repeated infinitely. The decision-maker selects $A^*$ where $\text{EMV}_{A^*} = \max_j \text{EMV}_j$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

If **perfect information** were available, the decision-maker would know in advance which state $S_i$ will occur and would choose the alternative yielding the highest payoff under that specific state.

$$
\text{Payoff under PI given state } S_i \;=\; \max_j V_{ij}
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{with PI}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \left(\max_j V_{ij}\right)
$$

The gain over the prior best decision is:

$$
\text{EVPI} \;=\; \text{EMV}_{\text{with PI}} - \max_j \text{EMV}_j
$$

**Interpretation:** EVPI represents the **maximum price** the decision-maker should be willing to pay for a perfect forecast (e.g., a flawless market research report or a 100%-accurate meteorological survey).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Bayes' Posterior Probability Update

Suppose prior probabilities $P(S_i)$ are known, and a sample or test $B$ is observed. The likelihood $P(B \mid S_i)$ of observing $B$ under each state is also known. The posterior probability $P(S_i \mid B)$ is:

$$
P(S_i \mid B) \;=\; \dfrac{P(B \mid S_i) \cdot P(S_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} P(B \mid S_j) \cdot P(S_j)}
$$

$$
P(S_i \mid B) \;=\; \dfrac{P(B \mid S_i) \cdot P(S_i)}{P(B_1) P(S_1) + P(B_2) P(S_2) + \dots + P(B_n) P(S_n)}
$$

**Logic:** The denominator is the total (marginal) probability of observing $B$, computed via the Law of Total Probability. The numerator is the joint probability of $S_i$ and $B$. Their ratio is the revised belief about $S_i$ after seeing evidence $B$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Decision Tree Construction with EMV

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A company must choose between constructing a **Large plant (L)** or a **Small plant (S)**. Market demand may be **High (H)** with probability **0.6** or **Low (L)** with probability **0.4**. Payoff matrix (₹ Lakhs):
>
> | | High Demand | Low Demand |
> |:---|:---:|:---:|
> | Large Plant | 200 | −80 |
> | Small Plant | 100 | 60 |

**Step 1 — Compute EMV of Large Plant:**

$$
\text{EMV}_L \;=\; (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(-80)
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_L \;=\; 120 - 32 \;=\; 88 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EMV of Small Plant:**

$$
\text{EMV}_S \;=\; (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(60)
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_S \;=\; 60 + 24 \;=\; 84 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision Rule:**

$$
A^* \;=\; \arg\max_j \text{EMV}_j \;=\; \arg\max \{88,\ 84\} \;=\; \text{Large Plant}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute EVPI (for analytical completeness):**

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{with PI}} \;=\; (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(60) \;=\; 120 + 24 \;=\; 144 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

$$
\text{EVPI} \;=\; 144 - 88 \;=\; 56 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Conclusion:** Choose the **Large Plant** with EMV = ₹88 Lakhs. The company should not pay more than ₹56 Lakhs for any perfect market forecast.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Criteria Under Uncertainty

When probabilities are **not known** (pure uncertainty), the following matrix applies:

| Criterion | Formula | Risk Profile | KTU 2024 Weight |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| Maximax (Optimistic) | $\max_j \max_i V_{ij}$ | Risk-Seeker | High |
| Maximin (Pessimistic) | $\max_j \min_i V_{ij}$ | Risk-Averse | High |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha$-index) | $\max_j \left[ \alpha \cdot \max_i V_{ij} + (1-\alpha) \cdot \min_i V_{ij} \right]$ | Balanced ($\alpha \in [0,1]$) | Medium |
| Laplace (Equally Likely) | $\max_j \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_i V_{ij}\right)$ | Neutral | High |
| Minimax Regret | $\min_j \max_i R_{ij}$ | Loss-Averse | Very High |

**Hurwicz Parameter Notes:** $\alpha = 1 \Rightarrow$ Maximax; $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow$ Maximin. KTU often gives $\alpha = 0.7$ or $\alpha = 0.3$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Generic Decision Tree Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["DECISION NODE A"]:::decision
    rootA --> branch1["Alternative 1"]
    rootA --> branch2["Alternative 2"]

    branch1 --> chance1A["CHANCE NODE B1"]:::chance
    branch2 --> chance2A["CHANCE NODE B2"]:::chance

    chance1A --> state1["S1: Payoff V11, Prob p1"]
    chance1A --> state2["S2: Payoff V12, Prob p2"]
    chance1A --> state3["S3: Payoff V13, Prob p3"]

    chance2A --> state4["S1: Payoff V21, Prob p1"]
    chance2A --> state5["S2: Payoff V22, Prob p2"]
    chance2A --> state6["S3: Payoff V23, Prob p3"]

    state1 --> emv1["EMV1 = Sum pi times Vi"]
    state4 --> emv2["EMV2 = Sum pi times Vi"]

    emv1 --> final["SELECT max EMV1 EMV2"]
    emv2 --> final

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chance fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Roll-Back (Fold-Back) Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start["Start: Decision Node"]:::decision
    branch["Draw decision branches"]
    chance["Attach chance nodes"]
    prob["Label probabilities pi"]
    payoff["Label payoffs Vij"]
    calcEMV["Compute EMVj = Sum pi Vij"]
    prop["Propagate EMV upward"]
    prune["Prune suboptimal branches"]
    opt["Optimal Decision A star"]

    start --> branch --> chance --> prob --> payoff --> calcEMV --> prop --> prune --> opt

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Bayesian Decision Tree Block (with Sample Information)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeD["DECISION: Take Sample Test"]:::decision
    nodeD --> testYes["Conduct Survey"]
    nodeD --> testNo["No Test: Use Prior EMV"]

    testYes --> nodeR1["Result Favorable R1"]:::chance
    testYes --> nodeR2["Result Unfavorable R2"]:::chance

    nodeR1 --> actionA1["Act: Large Plant"]
    nodeR1 --> actionA2["Act: Small Plant"]
    nodeR2 --> actionB1["Act: Large Plant"]
    nodeR2 --> actionB2["Act: Small Plant"]

    actionA1 --> payA1["Payoff VA1"]
    actionA2 --> payA2["Payoff VA2"]
    actionB1 --> payB1["Payoff VB1"]
    actionB2 --> payB2["Payoff VB2"]

    payA1 --> emvFinal["Roll back: Compute EMV with sample info"]
    payA2 --> emvFinal
    payB1 --> emvFinal
    payB2 --> emvFinal

    testNo --> priorEmv["Prior EMV best alternative"]
    emvFinal --> compare{"EVwSI greater than Prior EMV"}:::decision
    priorEmv --> compare
    compare -->|Yes| takeSurvey["Take the Survey"]
    compare -->|No| skipSurvey["Skip the Survey"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chance fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Decision Criteria Selection Block (Uncertainty Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ{"Are state probabilities known"}:::decision
    startQ -->|Yes Risk| riskPath["Use Expected Monetary Value EMV"]
    startQ -->|No Uncertainty| uncPath["Use Decision Criteria"]

    riskPath --> emvCalc["Compute EMV for each alternative"]
    emvCalc --> emvSelect["Select max EMV"]

    uncPath --> maxMax["Maximax: Optimistic"]
    uncPath --> maxMin["Maximin: Pessimistic"]
    uncPath --> hurw["Hurwicz: Weighted Alpha"]
    uncPath --> laplace["Laplace: Equal Weights"]
    uncPath --> regret["Minimax Regret: Loss Based"]

    maxMax --> compareAll["Compare results across criteria"]
    maxMin --> compareAll
    hurw --> compareAll
    laplace --> compareAll
    regret --> compareAll
    compareAll --> finalPick["Final Decision: Use Dominant Criterion"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
Distinguish clearly between **decision-making under risk** and **decision-making under uncertainty**. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Risk | Uncertainty |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Probabilities of outcomes | **Known** (objective or subjective) | **Unknown** or not quantifiable |
| Decision criterion | Expected Monetary Value (EMV) | Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz, Laplace, Minimax Regret |
| Example | Insurance premium based on actuarial mortality tables | Strategic investment in a new, untested technology with no historical data |

> Risk uses probabilistic analysis; uncertainty relies on heuristic criteria because numerical likelihoods are unavailable. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**  
What is the **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)**? State its formula and managerial significance.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** EVPI is the maximum additional expected return the decision-maker would gain by having *perfect foreknowledge* of which state of nature will occur before choosing an alternative.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{EVPI} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot \left(\max_j V_{ij}\right) \;-\; \max_j \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot V_{ij}
$$

**Significance:** EVPI represents the **upper monetary ceiling** on the price the decision-maker should be willing to pay for any external information source (market survey, expert opinion, prototype test). *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**  
A textile company is deciding whether to launch a **new product (N)** or continue with the **existing product (E)**. Market response can be **Favourable (F)** with probability **0.55** or **Unfavourable (U)** with probability **0.45**. Before launch, the company may commission a **market survey (S)** costing ₹5 Lakhs, with the following accuracy data:
- $P(\text{Survey Favourable} \mid \text{Market Favourable}) = 0.80$
- $P(\text{Survey Unfavourable} \mid \text{Market Unfavourable}) = 0.75$

| Decision × State | Favourable (₹ Lakhs) | Unfavourable (₹ Lakhs) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| New Product (N) | 300 | −120 |
| Existing Product (E) | 150 | 80 |

**(a)** Draw the decision tree and determine the **optimal decision without sample information**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Construct the **Bayesian decision tree with sample information** and decide whether the survey should be commissioned. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute EMV of New Product (N):**

$$
\text{EMV}_N \;=\; (0.55)(300) + (0.45)(-120) \;=\; 165 - 54 \;=\; 111 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute EMV of Existing Product (E):**

$$
\text{EMV}_E \;=\; (0.55)(150) + (0.45)(80) \;=\; 82.5 + 36 \;=\; 118.5 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision:**

$$
\max(111,\ 118.5) \;=\; 118.5 \text{ Lakhs} \Rightarrow A^* \;=\; \text{Existing Product (E)}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Tree Structure:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0{"Decision: Without Survey"}:::decision
    D0 --> newProd["New Product N"]
    D0 --> exiProd["Existing Product E"]

    newProd --> ch1{"Chance Node"}:::chance
    ch1 --> fav1["Favourable: 300, p=0.55"]
    ch1 --> unf1["Unfavourable: -120, p=0.45"]

    exiProd --> ch2{"Chance Node"}:::chance
    ch2 --> fav2["Favourable: 150, p=0.55"]
    ch2 --> unf2["Unfavourable: 80, p=0.45"]

    fav1 --> emvN["EMV N = 111"]
    unf1 --> emvN
    fav2 --> emvE["EMV E = 118.5"]
    unf2 --> emvE

    emvN --> best{"Select max"}:::decision
    emvE --> best
    best -->|E wins| opt["Choose Existing Product: EMV = 118.5 Lakhs"]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef chance fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Conclusion (a):** Without survey, choose **Existing Product (E)** with EMV = ₹118.5 Lakhs. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Joint and Marginal Probabilities of Survey Outcome:**

$$
P(S_F \cap M_F) \;=\; P(S_F \mid M_F) \cdot P(M_F) \;=\; (0.80)(0.55) \;=\; 0.44
$$

$$
P(S_F \cap M_U) \;=\; P(S_F \mid M_U) \cdot P(M_U) \;=\; (0.25)(0.45) \;=\; 0.1125
$$

$$
P(S_U \cap M_F) \;=\; P(S_U \mid M_F) \cdot P(M_F) \;=\; (0.20)(0.55) \;=\; 0.11
$$

$$
P(S_U \cap M_U) \;=\; P(S_U \mid M_U) \cdot P(M_U) \;=\; (0.75)(0.45) \;=\; 0.3375
$$

**Step 2 — Marginal Probabilities of Survey Outcome:**

$$
P(S_F) \;=\; 0.44 + 0.1125 \;=\; 0.5525
$$

$$
P(S_U) \;=\; 0.11 + 0.3375 \;=\; 0.4475
$$

**Step 3 — Posterior Probabilities (Bayes' Theorem):**

$$
P(M_F \mid S_F) \;=\; \dfrac{0.44}{0.5525} \;\approx\; 0.7964
$$

$$
P(M_U \mid S_F) \;=\; \dfrac{0.1125}{0.5525} \;\approx\; 0.2036
$$

$$
P(M_F \mid S_U) \;=\; \dfrac{0.11}{0.4475} \;\approx\; 0.2458
$$

$$
P(M_U \mid S_U) \;=\; \dfrac{0.3375}{0.4475} \;\approx\; 0.7542
$$

**Step 4 — Revised EMVs (Conditional on Survey Outcome):**

*If Survey Favourable ($S_F$):*

$$
\text{EMV}_{N \mid S_F} \;=\; (0.7964)(300) + (0.2036)(-120) \;=\; 238.92 - 24.43 \;=\; 214.49
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_{E \mid S_F} \;=\; (0.7964)(150) + (0.2036)(80) \;=\; 119.46 + 16.29 \;=\; 135.75
$$

$$
\text{Best if } S_F \;=\; \max(214.49,\ 135.75) \;=\; 214.49 \text{ (New Product)}
$$

*If Survey Unfavourable ($S_U$):*

$$
\text{EMV}_{N \mid S_U} \;=\; (0.2458)(300) + (0.7542)(-120) \;=\; 73.74 - 90.50 \;=\; -16.76
$$

$$
\text{EMV}_{E \mid S_U} \;=\; (0.2458)(150) + (0.7542)(80) \;=\; 36.87 + 60.34 \;=\; 97.21
$$

$$
\text{Best if } S_U \;=\; \max(-16.76,\ 97.21) \;=\; 97.21 \text{ (Existing Product)}
$$

**Step 5 — Expected Value with Sample Information (EVwSI):**

$$
\text{EVwSI} \;=\; P(S_F) \cdot (\text{Best} \mid S_F) + P(S_U) \cdot (\text{Best} \mid S_U)
$$

$$
\text{EVwSI} \;=\; (0.5525)(214.49) + (0.4475)(97.21)
$$

$$
\text{EVwSI} \;=\; 118.51 + 43.50 \;=\; 162.01 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 6 — Net Expected Gain from Survey (Subtract Cost):**

$$
\text{Net EVwSI} \;=\; 162.01 - 5.00 \;=\; 157.01 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

**Step 7 — Compare with Prior EMV:**

$$
157.01 \;>\; 118.5 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Commission the Survey}
$$

**Conclusion (b):** Yes, the survey should be commissioned. Net expected gain = **₹157.01 Lakhs** versus prior best of ₹118.5 Lakhs, a gain of **₹38.51 Lakhs**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024]**  
A software firm must choose between two investment projects, **P1** and **P2**, under three possible economic scenarios: **Boom (B)** with probability **0.3**, **Normal (N)** with probability **0.5**, and **Recession (R)** with probability **0.2**.

| Project | Boom (₹ Cr) | Normal (₹ Cr) | Recession (₹ Cr) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| P1 | 50 | 30 | 10 |
| P2 | 40 | 35 | 25 |

**(a)** Construct the decision tree and recommend the project using the **Expected Monetary Value** criterion. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Analyze the same decision under uncertainty (unknown probabilities) using **Maximax, Maximin, Hurwicz (α = 0.6), Laplace, and Minimax Regret** criteria. State the recommended action for each. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — EMV of P1:**

$$
\text{EMV}_{P1} \;=\; (0.3)(50) + (0.5)(30) + (0.2)(10) \;=\; 15 + 15 + 2 \;=\; 32 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 2 — EMV of P2:**

$$
\text{EMV}_{P2} \;=\; (0.3)(40) + (0.5)(35) + (0.2)(25) \;=\; 12 + 17.5 + 5 \;=\; 34.5 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommendation:**

$$
A^* \;=\; \arg\max\{32,\ 34.5\} \;=\; P2 \text{ with EMV} = ₹34.5 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Step 4 — EVPI (Bonus Computation):**

$$
\text{EMV}_{\text{PI}} \;=\; (0.3)(50) + (0.5)(35) + (0.2)(25) \;=\; 15 + 17.5 + 5 \;=\; 37.5
$$

$$
\text{EVPI} \;=\; 37.5 - 34.5 \;=\; 3 \text{ Cr}
$$

**Conclusion (a):** Choose **Project P2** with EMV = ₹34.5 Cr. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximax (Optimistic):**

$$
\max_i V_{i,P1} \;=\; 50,\quad \max_i V_{i,P2} \;=\; 40
$$

$$
\text{Maximax} \;=\; \max\{50,\ 40\} \;=\; 50 \Rightarrow P1
$$

**Step 2 — Maximin (Pessimistic):**

$$
\min_i V_{i,P1} \;=\; 10,\quad \min_i V_{i,P2} \;=\; 25
$$

$$
\text{Maximin} \;=\; \max\{10,\ 25\} \;=\; 25 \Rightarrow P2
$$

**Step 3 — Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$):**

$$
H_{P1} \;=\; (0.6)(50) + (0.4)(10) \;=\; 30 + 4 \;=\; 34
$$

$$
H_{P2} \;=\; (0.6)(40) + (0.4)(25) \;=\; 24 + 10 \;=\; 34
$$

$$
\text{Hurwicz} \;=\; \max\{34,\ 34\} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Tie (Both Equally Optimal)}
$$

**Step 4 — Laplace (Equal Probability 1/3):**

$$
L_{P1} \;=\; \frac{50 + 30 + 10}{3} \;=\; 30
$$

$$
L_{P2} \;=\; \frac{40 + 35 + 25}{3} \;=\; 33.33
$$

$$
\text{Laplace} \;=\; \max\{30,\ 33.33\} \;\Rightarrow\; P2
$$

**Step 5 — Minimax Regret:**

*Regret Table:* $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{ik} - V_{ij}$

- Column maxes: Boom = 50, Normal = 35, Recession = 25.
- For P1: Regrets = $(50-50, 35-30, 25-10) = (0, 5, 15)$; Max regret = 15.
- For P2: Regrets = $(50-40, 35-35, 25-25) = (10, 0, 0)$; Max regret = 10.

$$
\text{Minimax Regret} \;=\; \min\{15,\ 10\} \;\Rightarrow\; P2
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Criterion | Choice |
|:---|:---:|
| Maximax | P1 |
| Maximin | P2 |
| Hurwicz ($\alpha = 0.6$) | Tie (P1, P2) |
| Laplace | P2 |
| Minimax Regret | P2 |

**Conclusion (b):** Under uncertainty, **P2 dominates in 3 of 5 criteria**, making it the robust choice. *(7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract survey cost:** When computing net EVwSI, students often omit the survey cost, gaining partial credit only.
> 2. **Probability summation error:** $\sum p_i$ at any chance node MUST equal 1. A mismatch invites a 2-mark penalty.
> 3. **Posterior vs. Prior Confusion:** Posterior $P(M_i \mid S_j)$ is computed AFTER the survey; do not substitute prior probabilities in revised EMVs.
> 4. **Regret Table Mis-Construction:** Regret is computed *column-wise* (by state), not row-wise. This is the most frequent error.
> 5. **Pruning Errors:** When two EMVs are equal, do NOT arbitrarily prune. State "tie" explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Trees Module**

- **Risk** → Probabilities known → Use **EMV** roll-back.
- **Uncertainty** → Probabilities unknown → Use **Maximax / Maximin / Hurwicz / Laplace / Minimax Regret**.
- **Bayes' Theorem** is the *bridge* between prior and posterior probabilities; always verify $\sum P(S_j) = 1$ at the marginalization step.
- **EVPI** = Maximum justifiable spend on additional information.
- **EVwSI − Cost of Survey** = Net gain; commission survey only if Net gain > 0.
- **Efficiency of Sample Info** $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{EVSI}}{\text{EVPI}}$ lies in $[0, 1]$.
- **Square = Decision**, **Circle = Chance**, **Triangle = Terminal Payoff** — Universal notation.
- **Roll-back** proceeds from RIGHT to LEFT (terminal nodes → decision node).
- **Utility Theory** is invoked when monetary values fail to capture decision-maker's risk attitude (especially for high-stakes irreversible decisions).
- **Hurwicz Parameter:** $\alpha \to 1$ → Optimistic; $\alpha \to 0$ → Pessimistic.
- **Regret = Opportunity Loss** = Difference between best payoff in a state and the actual payoff.
- **Decision Dominance:** If one alternative is uniformly better across all states, it is automatically optimal — no criterion required.
- **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** = $\sum p_i \cdot R_{ij}$. Minimizing EOL is equivalent to maximizing EMV.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EMV Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT865 - STATISTICAL AND COMPUTATIONAL METHODS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** EMV Method

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Expected Monetary Value (EMV) Method** is a quantitative decision-making technique used in decision analysis under **conditions of risk**, where the decision-maker can assign numerical probabilities to each possible **state of nature** (future event) but does not have full control over which state will actually occur.

In the EMV framework, every alternative course of action (decision strategy) is assigned a single numerical value — its **Expected Monetary Value** — computed as the **probability-weighted sum of all possible payoff outcomes** (profits, losses, or costs) associated with that alternative across every conceivable state of nature.

Mathematically, if an alternative $A_i$ has payoffs $X_{ij}$ against states of nature $S_j$ (with probabilities $P_j$), then:

$$\text{EMV}(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot X_{ij}$$

The decision-maker selects the alternative that **maximizes EMV** (in profit-oriented problems) or **minimizes EMV** (in cost-oriented problems).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Umbrella Decision:**
> Imagine you are leaving home each morning during the monsoon season. You face a decision: **Carry an umbrella** or **Don't carry an umbrella**. You know from past weather data that:
> - Probability of **rain** = **0.30** → Carrying umbrella saves you ₹200 (no wet clothes); not carrying costs you ₹200.
> - Probability of **no rain** = **0.70** → Carrying umbrella wastes ₹50 (inconvenience cost); not carrying saves ₹50.
>
> If you *could* predict tomorrow's weather with certainty, the decision would be trivial (a deterministic problem). However, since you only have **probabilities**, you are operating under **risk**.
>
> **EMV computes the "long-run average" payoff** of each strategy if you repeated this same decision many times under identical probability conditions. The alternative with the highest long-run average is your rational choice.

### 1.3 Classification of Decision Environments

To anchor the EMV method, it must be positioned within the broader taxonomy of decision-making environments:

| Environment | Probabilities Known? | States of Nature Certain? | Typical Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Certainty** | Yes (deterministic) | Yes (one state) | Simple Optimization / LP |
| **Risk** | **Yes (objective/subjective)** | **No (multiple)** | **EMV, EOL, EPPI** |
| **Uncertainty** | No | No | Maximax, Maximin, Laplace, Hurwicz, Savage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** EMV falls strictly under the **"Decision Making Under Risk"** category. If probabilities are absent, EMV cannot be computed, and the student must shift to decision-making under uncertainty techniques.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Vocabulary

- **State of Nature ($S_j$):** A future event or condition beyond the decision-maker's control (e.g., demand = high/medium/low).
- **Alternative / Act / Decision ($A_i$):** The controllable course of action available to the decision-maker.
- **Payoff ($X_{ij}$):** The monetary outcome (profit, cost, or loss) resulting from choosing $A_i$ when $S_j$ occurs.
- **Probability ($P_j$):** The likelihood that state $S_j$ will occur, where $\sum P_j = 1$.
- **Payoff Matrix:** A structured tabular representation of all $X_{ij}$ values.
- **Expected Value (Expectation):** The probability-weighted arithmetic mean of a random variable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Architecture of EMV — Six Stepwise Logic Blocks

The EMV decision procedure can be decomposed into six sequential, reproducible logic blocks:

**Step 1 — Problem Structuring (Identification Phase):**
The decision-maker enumerates the complete set of feasible alternatives $A_i \in \{A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_m\}$ and the exhaustive, mutually exclusive set of possible states of nature $S_j \in \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_n\}$.

**Step 2 — Payoff Matrix Construction:**
For every pair $(A_i, S_j)$, the monetary outcome $X_{ij}$ is determined and recorded, forming an $m \times n$ payoff matrix. In cost problems, this is called a **cost matrix**; in profit problems, a **profit matrix**.

**Step 3 — Probability Assignment:**
Each state $S_j$ is assigned a probability $P_j \geq 0$, with the fundamental probability axiom enforced:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j = 1$$

Probabilities may be **objective** (derived from historical frequency data) or **subjective** (expert judgment, Bayesian inference).

**Step 4 — EMV Computation:**
For each alternative $A_i$, the EMV is computed as the inner product of the payoff row $X_i = (X_{i1}, X_{i2}, \ldots, X_{in})$ with the probability vector $P = (P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n)$:

$$\text{EMV}(A_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_j \cdot X_{ij}$$

**Step 5 — Optimal Alternative Selection:**
- For **profit-oriented** problems, select $A^*$ such that $\text{EMV}(A^*) = \max_i \text{EMV}(A_i)$.
- For **cost-oriented** problems, select $A^*$ such that $\text{EMV}(A^*) = \min_i \text{EMV}(A_i)$.

**Step 6 — Sensitivity / Post-Optimality Check (KTU Exam Favorite):**
Examine how robust the optimal choice is to perturbations in $P_j$. A decision is **robust** if small changes in probabilities do not alter the choice.

### 2.2 Underlying Theoretical Foundations — The "Why"

> [!NOTE]
> **The Law of Large Numbers (LLN) Justification:**
> The EMV method derives its theoretical legitimacy from the **Weak Law of Large Numbers**. If the decision situation is repeated $N$ times (large $N$), the **sample average payoff converges in probability** to the EMV.
